case_text:
A 51-year-old man presented with a 10-year history of pain in the plantar aspect of his right foot and a 4-year history of progressive swelling between the first and second metatarsal shafts. He had no prior surgery or trauma, denied constitutional symptoms, and was otherwise healthy. He had taken NSAIDs for pain relief, but over the past year the pain began to limit daily activities despite analgesia.

On examination, there was a 6 × 3 cm irregular, erythematous, firm, nonmobile, tender, and warm swelling between the first and second metatarsal shafts, extending plantarly and medially over the first metatarsal. Ankle plantarflexion and dorsiflexion were normal and pain-free, and subtalar inversion and eversion were normal. Range of motion at the first and second metatarsophalangeal joints was limited by pain. Neurovascular examination was intact, and gait was antalgic.

Plain radiographs of the right foot showed soft-tissue swelling with a 4 × 3 cm area of calcification between the first and third metatarsal shafts without bone involvement. MRI demonstrated a large, nonhomogeneous mass between the first and third metatarsal shafts extending plantarly, with mixed fibrous tissue and bony structures.

A 4-year-old Afghan refugee girl was admitted with 3 days of intermittent periumbilical and hypogastric abdominal pain occurring every 60 minutes and lasting 20 minutes, accompanied by two episodes of bilious vomiting (last night and 1 hour before admission) and one episode of currant-jelly stool without hematochezia. Self-treatment with anticholinergics and antiemetics provided temporary relief. She had no prior medical or surgical history. 

On examination, she appeared ill, with abdominal distention and a palpable, tender mass in the periumbilical area. 

Laboratory tests showed a leukocyte count of 15,600 cells/µL (neutrophils 12,948 cells/µL) and a C-reactive protein level of 4.4 mg/dL. Urinalysis revealed 4+ ketones, 4–6 WBCs/hpf, 0–1 epithelial cells/hpf, and a few bacteria. 

Abdominal ultrasonography demonstrated an ileoileal invagination with a “doughnut” sign, obstructive features, free abdominopelvic fluid, and absent vascular flow in the invaginated loop on Doppler imaging.

A 50-year-old woman with a >10-year history of mid-dorsal back pain noted progressive lower-limb weakness and stiffness over 8 months, worse on the right, requiring a walker. She also reported bilateral thigh paresthesias and urinary hesitancy. On examination, she had right hip and knee flexion 3/5, foot dorsiflexion and plantar flexion 2/5; left lower-limb strength was 4/5. There was a sensory level at T8 with hypoesthesia below, severely impaired position sense in the right leg, absent abdominal reflexes, hyperreflexia, and bilateral Babinski signs. Anal tone was preserved with weak voluntary contraction. MRI of the thoracic spine showed a fusiform posterior epidural mass spanning T6–T9, compressing the cord. The lesion was inhomogeneous, hyperintense on T1- and T2-weighted images, and showed inhomogeneous enhancement on postcontrast T1-weighted images with and without fat suppression. It tapered at both ends and extended through the neural foramina into the thoracic cavity.

A 44-year-old woman presented with a 1-week history of lower abdominal pain and a “feeling of pressure in the rectum” during bowel movements associated with an increased urge to defecate. She had not been sexually active for more than 10 years. On examination, she was afebrile; her lower abdomen was tender bilaterally, and cervical motion tenderness was elicited. Laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 12.4×10^9 per liter and a C-reactive protein level of 62 mg per liter; a pregnancy test was negative. She was diagnosed with pelvic inflammatory disease and started on intravenous broad-spectrum antibiotics.  
  A CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis showed two cystic structures in the pelvis. A transvaginal ultrasound demonstrated a 5.8-cm unilocular simple cyst in the right ovary and a 3-cm left tubo-ovarian complex with associated hydrosalpinx. Ovarian tumor markers, blood cultures, vaginal swabs, and tests for sexually transmitted infections were negative.  
  The patient’s symptoms and laboratory values improved, and on hospital day 3, she was discharged on oral metronidazole and doxycycline with outpatient follow-up. Three months later, a repeat ultrasound showed a persistent 5-cm simple cyst of the right ovary and a left hydrosalpinx; she continued to have intermittent lower abdominal pain. Given the ongoing pain and imaging findings, she was scheduled for a bilateral salpingectomy and right ovarian cystectomy.

A 92‐year‐old woman was referred to vascular surgery for an enlarging hematoma of the left thigh. Two months earlier, she had undergone dynamic hip screw fixation for an intertrochanteric femoral fracture. On admission, she was febrile (temperature >38°C), mildly anemic (hematocrit 32.9%, hemoglobin 10.7 g/dL), but hemodynamically stable with palpable peripheral pulses in both lower extremities. During the postoperative period, despite repeated blood transfusions, her hematocrit and hemoglobin levels declined progressively to 22.5% and 7.1 g/dL, respectively. Serial ultrasound examinations showed the thigh hematoma enlarging from an initial diameter of 9 cm to over 20 cm. By postoperative day 25, the hematoma enlargement had plateaued and there were no signs of lower‐extremity ischemia, leading to her discharge. On representation, the hematoma continued to be a concern, prompting further evaluation.

An 8-month-old, previously healthy female (9 kg) underwent emergent drainage of a retropharyngeal and anterior mediastinal abscess and thoracoscopic decortication of a right-sided empyema. Intraoperatively, she had brief episodes of hypoxia with associated bradycardia that resolved with manual ventilation. At the conclusion of the procedure, her oxygen saturation fell to 40% and she developed minimal breath sounds and progressive hypercarbia. Ventilator settings were adjusted without improvement; tidal volumes remained at 15–30 mL despite peak inspiratory pressures > 50 cm H₂O. Attempts to pass a suction catheter through the 4.0 mm endotracheal tube were unsuccessful. The available fiberoptic scope and exchange catheters could not be advanced through the tube. An intraoperative chest radiograph confirmed correct placement of the endotracheal tube.

A 36-year-old South Asian woman with systemic lupus erythematosus complicated by class IV lupus nephritis and hypertension, in clinical remission on prednisolone, mycophenolate mofetil, hydroxychloroquine, and antihypertensives, received her first dose of intravenous rituximab (375 mg/m2). Ten days later, she presented with one day of spontaneous oral and nasal bleeding and new patchy ecchymoses on her trunk and extremities. She had no other complaints.  
On examination, she had oozing blood from the gums, two blood-filled blisters on the hard palate, blood on the alar nares without crusting, and multiple ecchymotic patches. The spleen tip was palpable. The rest of the examination was unremarkable.  
Initial laboratory studies showed:  
• Platelet count 5 × 10^9/L (150–450)  
• Hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL (11–15)  
• White-cell count 6.8 × 10^9/L (4–11)  
• Serum creatinine 98 μmol/L (44–97)  
• Serum albumin 4.03 g/dL  
• INR 0.92 (<1.0)  
• C-reactive protein 0.6 mg/dL (<5.0)  
• Direct Coombs test negative  
Blood smear confirmed severe thrombocytopenia without schistocytes or abnormal cells.

A 21-year-old man presented after an unwitnessed syncope complicated by a right femoral neck fracture. Laboratory evaluation revealed an albumin-corrected serum calcium of 4.9 mg/dL (reference 8.9–10.3). He denied paresthesias, muscle spasms, palpitations, or neurologic symptoms. He took no medications or supplements. 

His medical history was notable for a neonatal Nissen fundoplication, a 15-cm proximal small-bowel resection at age 9 for adhesive obstruction, and a hemigastrectomy at age 12 for gastric perforation. Pediatric records also noted an intertrochanteric brown tumor of the right femur at age 9. 

On examination, he was thin with well-healed abdominal scars. The right hip and thigh were tender with decreased range of motion; the leg was held in external rotation. Chvostek and Trousseau signs were negative. There were no skeletal deformities, and his height and weight were normal.

Initial biochemical studies showed:  
• Parathyroid hormone 273 pg/mL (12–88)  
• 25-hydroxyvitamin D 28 ng/mL (30–100)  
• 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D 54 pg/mL (18–72)  
• Magnesium 2.1 mg/dL (1.5–2.6)  
• Phosphorus 4.4 mg/dL (2.4–4.7)  
• Normal renal function  

He was treated with intravenous calcium gluconate followed by high-dose oral calcium carbonate and calcitriol. His serum calcium normalized while on the infusion but fell again to 6.1 mg/dL after the infusion was stopped, despite ongoing oral therapy. Doses of calcium carbonate and calcitriol were progressively increased, yet serum calcium remained below the reference range. Further testing revealed:  
• Hemoglobin 12.0 g/dL (13.2–17.7)  
• Folate 6.7 ng/mL (≥7.8)  
• Vitamin B12 286 pg/mL (180–914)  
• Ferritin normal  
• Negative celiac serologies  

Given the history of partial gastrectomy and bowel resection, persistent hypocalcemia on oral calcium carbonate, and evidence of micronutrient deficiencies, malabsorption was suspected.

A 27-year-old woman presented with a 2-week history of progressive decreased vision in her left eye, which began shortly after a flu-like illness. She had no other systemic symptoms or past medical history. Best corrected visual acuity was 20/400 in the left eye and 20/20 in the right. Anterior-segment examination was normal bilaterally. Fundus examination of the left eye showed cystoid macular edema, a serous (neurosensory) detachment at the macula, flame-shaped retinal hemorrhages inferior to the fovea, hard exudates at the margins of the detachment, and two juxtafoveal yellowish lesions located nasal and temporal to the fovea. The peripheral retina was unremarkable. The right fundus was normal. Fluorescein angiography of the left eye revealed early blockage of background fluorescence by the superficial hemorrhages with patchy mild hyperfluorescence, and in the late and very late phases, diffuse leakage at the level of the retinal pigment epithelium under the hemorrhages with pooling of dye in the subretinal space. Optical coherence tomography of the left macula demonstrated cystoid macular edema with multilayered cystic spaces, neurosensory detachment, and two hyperreflective juxtafoveal globular lesions in the outer retina; there was no evidence of choroidal neovascularization. Laboratory studies, including complete blood count, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, C-reactive protein, tuberculin skin test, angiotensin-converting enzyme level, rapid plasma reagin, antinuclear antibodies, and IgM antibodies against Bartonella and Toxoplasma, were all negative.
predicted_diagnoses: ['multiple evanescent white dot syndrome', 'acute multifocal placoid pigment epitheliopathy', 'toxoplasmosis']

case_text:
A 77-year-old woman was referred after an abnormal opacification was noted on routine chest radiography. She was asymptomatic and had no history of cutaneous lesions or occupational exposure to silica, beryllium, or asbestos. 
On chest computed tomography, there was a solitary, well-demarcated, solid nodule measuring 2.3 cm in the right lower lobe. Systemic CT revealed no other tumors. 
The patient underwent video-assisted thoracoscopic wedge resection of the right lower lobe. Intraoperatively, the nodule was firm and solid; on sectioning, it appeared yellow-white. A frozen section showed proliferation of fibromuscular tissue with a few mitotic figures, suggesting a mesenchymal tumor, prompting completion of a right lower lobectomy. 
Permanent histopathology demonstrated a well-circumscribed nodular lesion in the pulmonary parenchyma, composed of bland spindle or oval cells arranged in storiform and loose fascicular patterns. Numerous branching or anastomosing blood vessels with hemangiopericytomatous features were present, and occasional mitoses were seen.

A 50-year-old woman presented with an erythematous, pruritic rash over her upper chest and neck of one month’s duration. The lesions appeared one week after the second dose of the inactivated SARS-CoV-2 vaccine and were preceded by similar but milder lesions three weeks earlier after the first vaccine dose. Her history was notable for left-sided invasive ductal breast carcinoma treated 15 years ago with radical mastectomy, chemotherapy, and radiotherapy, with no recurrence since.

On examination, there was a relatively ill-defined, erythematous plaque with focal scaling and mild lichenification over the upper chest in the area corresponding to her prior radiation field, as marked by a tattoo.

A punch biopsy of the lesion revealed epidermal hyperkeratosis, parakeratosis, mild irregular acanthosis, mild spongiosis, focal basal cell degeneration with Civatte bodies, and numerous apoptotic keratinocytes. The upper dermis showed perivascular lymphocytic and eosinophilic infiltrates, dilated blood vessels, and scattered mast cells.

A 45-year-old woman presented with 9 months of progressive virilization including centripetal obesity, hirsutism, facial acne, hypertension, and oligomenorrhea. She noted a gradually enlarging, palpable mass in her right upper quadrant. Laboratory studies showed markedly elevated dehydroepiandrosterone, dehydroepiandrosterone-sulfate, cortisol, and testosterone levels consistent with a functional suprarenal tumor. Abdominal ultrasonography and noncontrast CT revealed a 16.2 × 13.2 × 12.7 cm solid mass occupying most of the right liver lobe, displacing the right kidney downward. Contrast-enhanced CT demonstrated heterogeneous enhancement of the lesion, stenosis of the inferior vena cava from the left renal vein to the hepatic veins, and no thrombus in the portal vein.
predicted_diagnoses: ['primary adrenal cortical carcinoma', 'primary hepatocellular carcinoma', 'metastatic adrenal cortical carcinoma']

case_text:
A 14-year-old boy with known ataxia telangiectasia presented with one month of redness and swelling of the left lower eyelid and medial canthus that failed to improve despite topical ciprofloxacin and erythromycin, intravenous ampicillin plus vancomycin, and oral linezolid and co-amoxiclav. He had no systemic symptoms (no fever, weight loss, night sweats, fatigue, abdominal cramps, or cough). He was wheelchair dependent due to his neurologic condition. 

On ophthalmic examination, visual acuity was 20/20 in the right eye and 8/20 in the left eye. Intraocular pressure was 14 mm Hg in the right eye; measurement in the left eye was not possible. There was swelling and erythema of the left inferior eyelid and medial canthus with an ulcerative lesion in the medial canthus. Ocular motility of the left eye showed a –2 limitation in downgaze and adduction. Funduscopic and slit-lamp examinations of both eyes were otherwise normal. No regional lymphadenopathy was palpable. 

Laboratory studies, including a complete blood count, were within normal limits. Because of his history of ataxia telangiectasia, CT was avoided and an MRI was obtained. Coronal T1- and T2-weighted images showed an infiltrative mass involving the inferior surface of the left orbit, the left ethmoidal sinus, and the left maxillary sinus, with extension toward the contralateral ethmoidal sinus. Gadolinium-enhanced sequences revealed marked enhancement of the lesion.

A 5-year-old girl was referred for failure to thrive. She was the fifth child of unrelated parents from the same village in southern Lebanon. Born at term via vaginal delivery, her birth weight was 2200 g; the pregnancy was uneventful, and there was no maternal medication use, smoking, or alcohol intake. Her perinatal course was notable for neonatal jaundice requiring phototherapy. Family history included mental retardation in a maternal uncle and a congenital heart defect in an older brother.

In early infancy, she had feeding difficulties, failure to thrive, recurrent respiratory infections, and eczematous skin lesions. Since birth, her urine had a musty odor, although repeated urine analyses were normal. Developmentally, she was hyperactive with significant speech delay (vocabulary of 10–15 words; few two-word sentences); gross and fine motor skills were age-appropriate, and she understood simple commands but had a short attention span and impulsive behavior.

On examination, her height was 84 cm, weight 10 kg, and head circumference 44 cm (all <3rd percentile). She had fair, dry skin and sparse fine blond hair. Facial features included an elongated face with a high sloping forehead, sparse lateral eyebrows, long eyelashes, telecanthus with hypertelorism, low-set ears, a broad nasal bridge with prominent tip, micrognathia, a thin upper lip, a high-arched palate, dental caries, and a sacral dimple. Eczematous changes were present mainly in the flexural areas of the extremities. The remainder of the physical examination was normal.

Laboratory studies revealed normal results for: complete blood count; blood urea nitrogen; creatinine; electrolytes; total serum protein; liver function tests; lipid profile; thyroid hormones; urine organic acids; plasma acylcarnitine and free carnitine; plasma amino acids; serum lactate and pyruvate; plasma zinc; immunoglobulins (including IgE); urinalysis and cultures; stool studies; insulin-like growth factor 1; anti-endomysial antibodies; sweat chloride; and a 46,XX karyotype. MRI of the brain, spinal radiographs, voiding cystourethrogram, and upper gastrointestinal series were all normal. Echocardiography showed only trace mitral regurgitation. Bone-age assessment was delayed by two years.

A 56-year-old Malaysian woman with a history of anemia presented with 1 month of bilateral lower extremity swelling, early satiety, and abdominal discomfort. She reported worsening edema when standing and had gained 10 pounds from fluid retention despite feeling thinner. She denied chest pain, dyspnea, alcohol or tobacco use, and had no significant family history. She was on oral iron and omeprazole; a colonoscopy 1 year earlier was normal. A positive H. pylori breath test led to triple therapy and an endoscopy at an outside hospital that was suggestive of a gastric mass.

On admission, temperature was 37°C, blood pressure 134/78 mmHg, pulse 79/min, respiratory rate 18/min, and oxygen saturation 100% on room air. Cardiovascular examination revealed a grade 3/6 systolic ejection murmur at the left lower sternal border. The abdomen was soft, non-tender, with normoactive bowel sounds. Both lower extremities had 2+ non-pitting edema to the thighs. No lymphadenopathy or skin rashes were noted.

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 8.5 g/dL (MCV 71.2 fL), reticulocyte count 4.36%, ferritin 32 ng/mL, transferrin 403 μg/dL, transferrin saturation 3%, serum iron 13 μg/dL, platelets 519,000/μL, normal white-cell count, sodium 134 mmol/L, AST 122 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 478 U/L, and albumin 2.3 g/dL.

Electrocardiogram demonstrated T-wave inversions in leads V1–V4 and an S1Q3T3 pattern; troponin was negative. A duplex ultrasound of the lower extremities revealed no deep vein thrombosis. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis showed severe intrahepatic narrowing of the inferior vena cava due to mass effect and an ill-defined 3.0×2.9 cm necrotic mass in the gastric antrum, with omental implants, nodular extension into the lesser sac, and a small amount of ascites.

A 15-year-old previously healthy African boy presented with sudden onset dyspnea without chest pain. Two weeks earlier he had developed progressive exertional dyspnea, malaise, and a >6 kg weight loss, but no fever or other constitutional symptoms. On examination he was tachycardic (116 beats/min), tachypneic (36 breaths/min), and pale, with scattered crackles in both lung fields and clinical evidence of a left-sided pneumothorax. Chest radiography confirmed a left pneumothorax and showed extensive bilateral nodules suggestive of metastases. A left intercostal drain was placed, improving his respiratory status.

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin of 4.7 g/dL with iron-deficiency and anemia of chronic disease indices; white cell and platelet counts were normal, and no atypical cells were seen on blood smear. Tumor markers (alpha-fetoprotein, beta-hCG, CA 19-9) were not elevated, and an HIV test was negative. Contrast-enhanced CT of the chest and abdomen revealed diffuse well-defined pulmonary nodules and a filling defect within the right atrium; other solid organs appeared normal. Thyroid and testicular ultrasound and a skeletal survey were unremarkable.

Transthoracic echocardiography showed a large, inhomogeneous, broad-based mass at the junction of the inferior vena cava and right atrium with frond-like projections prolapsing through the tricuspid valve and a small circumferential pericardial effusion.

A 46‐year‐old woman recuperating in the ICU after laparotomy for ovarian malignancy experienced acute breathlessness and chest discomfort 20–25 minutes after receiving a single unit of packed red blood cells.  
On examination: temperature 100°F; heart rate 140–144/min; blood pressure 85/48 mm Hg; respiratory rate >30/min; oxygen saturation <80% on supplemental oxygen.  
Chest radiograph revealed extensive bilateral pulmonary infiltrates.  
Arterial blood gas on 10 L/min O₂ by nasal prongs showed pH 7.447, pCO₂ 23.8 mm Hg, pO₂ 30.7 mm Hg, SpO₂ 62.2%; hemoglobin 10.4 g/dL, hematocrit 32%.  
Invasive monitoring: central venous pressure 12 mm Hg.  
Electrocardiogram: sinus tachycardia (144/min) without ischemic changes.  
Transthoracic echocardiogram: normal study, LVEF 55%, no regional wall-motion abnormalities.  
Troponin T 0.352 ng/mL (normal 0–0.2), CK-MB 47 IU/L (normal 0–23); D-dimer within normal limits.
predicted_diagnoses: ['transfusion related acute lung injury', 'acute respiratory distress syndrome', 'pulmonary embolism']

case_text:
A 2-day-old male infant, born at home by spontaneous vaginal delivery at 38 weeks’ gestation and weighing 2.5 kg, was referred for abdominal distension, failure to pass meconium, and bilious vomiting since birth. According to the mother, the neonate was born with a distended abdomen and had passed white “pellets” per rectum shortly after delivery. The abdominal distension increased progressively, and each attempt at feeding was followed by bilious emesis.  

On examination, the infant appeared lethargic and in respiratory distress, with visible peristaltic waves across a distended abdomen. Vital signs showed a temperature of 100 °F, respiratory rate of 45 breaths per minute, and heart rate of 150 beats per minute. A digital rectal examination revealed multiple meconium beads.  

A preliminary diagnosis of neonatal intestinal obstruction secondary to meconium ileus was made, with distal intestinal atresia in the differential. The infant was resuscitated with intravenous fluids, given vitamin K, started on broad-spectrum antibiotics, and nasogastric decompression was instituted.  

An abdominal radiograph demonstrated markedly dilated small-bowel loops. A contrast enema with gastrografin revealed a microcolon and multiple intraluminal filling defects consistent with meconium beads. Despite enemas, the patient’s abdominal distension worsened and clinical status deteriorated, prompting decision for exploratory laparotomy.

A 24-year-old Caucasian woman was admitted with pharmacoresistant epilepsy. Her seizures began at age 11 as complex partial atonic events; within months she developed cognitive and behavioral decline. Multiple antiepileptic drugs failed to control her seizures, which occurred daily.

Initial CT of the head showed a left temporoparietal diffuse hypodense area that was inhomogeneous and lacked mass effect. Four years later MRI demonstrated a 32.3 × 43.1 × 28.3 mm multicystic cortico–subcortical parietal lesion divided by septations, with no edema, no mass effect, and no contrast enhancement. On admission 13 years after seizure onset, neurologic examination was normal except for neuropsychological deficits; laboratory tests, chest radiograph, and cardiac evaluation were unremarkable. Tc99m-MIBI single-photon emission CT of the brain showed no tumor metabolic activity. Electroencephalography revealed interictal spikes and polyspikes, and one minute of hyperventilation provoked a generalized tonic–clonic seizure recorded on EEG.

A 57-year-old woman was evaluated for mild direct hyperbilirubinemia and recurrent cholangitis. Her history was notable for hypertension and a prior laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Initial abdominal ultrasonography demonstrated small stones in the common bile duct. She underwent elective ERCP, during which biliary stones were removed and a plastic stent was placed. Cholangiography also revealed a distal bile duct stenosis, thought to be caused by extrinsic compression from a cystic lesion in the head of the pancreas. Serum CA 19-9 was 343 U/mL (normal < 40). Subsequent MRI with cholangiographic sequences showed a 3-cm cystic cluster in the pancreatic head responsible for biliary compression. Endoscopic ultrasound confirmed an IPMN-like cystic lesion but did not demonstrate communication with the pancreatic ducts; there were no worrisome features or solid components. After multidisciplinary review, the patient was referred for surgical resection.
predicted_diagnoses: ['intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasm (ipmn)', 'chronic pancreatitis', 'biliary sphincter of oddi dysfunction']

case_text: A 49-year-old woman presented with a 7-year history of multiple masses in her right palm and wrist and 6 months of progressive numbness in the thumb, index, and middle fingers. She recalled an injection over the wrist crease 7 years ago but did not know its contents. On examination, there was a 2 × 3 cm mass in the thenar area and a 3 × 4 cm mass proximal to the radiocarpal joint. Sensation was decreased in the median nerve distribution, and Tinel’s sign over the carpal tunnel was positive; thenar muscle bulk was preserved. Plain radiographs showed no bony abnormalities or calcifications. Nerve conduction studies were consistent with moderate carpal tunnel syndrome. MRI of the forearm and hand revealed fusiform enlargement of the median nerve extending from the distal forearm into the palm. On axial T1-weighted images, low-intensity nerve fascicles were surrounded by high-intensity fat, producing a “coaxial cable-like” appearance; on coronal and sagittal sequences, the nerve bundles appeared “spaghetti-like” within adipose tissue.

An 83-year-old man with no significant medical history was referred for home medical care (HMC) 70 days after an intensive care admission for airway obstruction and respiratory compromise. After discharge, he required assistance for daily activities; he could eat soft meals, use the toilet independently, but could no longer climb stairs. On initial HMC evaluation, vital signs were blood pressure 102/55 mmHg, heart rate 63 bpm, respiratory rate 20/min, temperature 35.3°C, and oxygen saturation 98%. Physical examination was unremarkable. An enteral nutritional supplement was prescribed, but adherence was poor.

Five days later, his family noted behavioral changes including tearing clothing and scratching furniture, initially attributed to relocation stress. At a follow-up visit 28 days after HMC initiation, he was restless and disoriented, unable to answer “Where are we?” Vital signs were blood pressure 126/55 mmHg, heart rate 65 bpm, respiratory rate 16/min, temperature 35.4°C, and oxygen saturation 98%. Examination revealed a dry tongue and skin. He ambulated with handrails due to lower extremity weakness. His clinical picture fulfilled DSM-5 criteria for delirium. 

“We considered dehydration due to poor oral intake, electrolyte abnormalities, side effects of drugs, chronic subdural hematoma, etc.,” and blood studies were obtained. “VB1 deficiency was also taken into consideration as the cause of delirium based on the fact that a family member had reported that his oral intake had decreased to 30% of normal.”
predicted_diagnoses: ['delirium due to vitamin b1 deficiency', 'chronic subdural hematoma', 'electrolyte abnormalities']

case_text:
A 69-year-old man was referred for evaluation of hilar bile duct stenosis. His history was significant for laparoscopic sigmoidectomy 4 years earlier for stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with synchronous liver metastases. He underwent partial hepatectomy of segment 7 and radiofrequency ablation of segment 8 lesions, and, two years later, resection of a local post-RFA recurrence. He had received no adjuvant chemotherapy.

On presentation, vital signs were within normal limits. Laboratory studies showed cholestatic liver enzyme elevation. Enhanced CT demonstrated wall thickening and enhancement of the hilar bile duct. Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography, endoscopic retrograde cholangiography, and intraductal ultrasound revealed a mass protruding into the lumen of the hilar bile duct. Brush cytology returned class IV cells. Based on imaging and cytology, a hilar cholangiocarcinoma (Bismuth type II) was diagnosed. The patient underwent right portal vein embolization followed by extended right hemihepatectomy with extrahepatic bile duct resection.

Gross examination revealed a 3 cm tumor in the hilar bile duct wall. Microscopic evaluation showed a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma involving the common hepatic duct wall with perineural invasion, but without vascular invasion, lymph node metastasis, or intraepithelial neoplasia at the tumor margin.

A 45-day-old girl, the second child of nonconsanguineous parents (an older sibling died at 3 months of unknown cause), was brought with 2 weeks of progressive abdominal distension and a generalized skin rash. Her growth parameters were age-appropriate. On examination she was pale and had scattered petechiae. The liver edge was palpable 4 cm below the right costal margin, and the spleen edge was 7 cm below the left costal margin. Other systems, including a normal funduscopic examination, were unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed: hemoglobin 6.8 g/dL, white-cell count 43,900/mm3 (30% neutrophils, 57% lymphocytes, 3% eosinophils, 9% monocytes), platelets 30,000/mm3; peripheral smear demonstrated leukoerythroblastosis; reticulocyte count 6%. Liver and renal function tests were normal. Serum calcium was 9.0 mg/dL, phosphorus 3.7 mg/dL, alkaline phosphatase 370 U/L, and lactate dehydrogenase 640 U/L. Retroviral studies and TORCH titers were negative; blood cultures were sterile. Abdominal ultrasonography confirmed hepatosplenomegaly.

On hospital day 3 she developed a cough. A chest radiograph incidentally showed diffuse skeletal sclerosis, and dedicated skeletal films revealed a characteristic “bone-in-bone” appearance in the femora, tibiae, humeri, radii, ulnae, and iliac bones.

A 40-year-old man presented with a 2-week history of chronic abdominal pain accompanied by diarrhea and generalized weakness. An abdominal ultrasonogram showed an inflamed appendix. On examination, there was localized tenderness and rebound in the right iliac fossa at McBurney’s point. The total leukocyte count was elevated at 16,000/mm^3, and other routine biochemical and hematological tests were within normal limits. A clinical diagnosis of chronic appendicitis was made, and the patient underwent an appendectomy. Intraoperatively, the appendix appeared inflamed and was studded with several small tubercles on the serosal surface and adjacent periappendiceal fat. The appendix and a mesenteric lymph node were submitted for histopathological examination.

A 33-year-old G4P3013 woman with a recent pregnancy of unknown location initially managed with two doses of methotrexate in April 2018 had a slow decline of β-hCG to normal levels. In June 2018 she was found to have a pulmonary arteriovenous malformation and underwent coil embolization. She was followed with weekly β-hCG levels until they were undetectable in July 2018, then was lost to follow-up.

In October 2018 she presented with right shoulder pain, abdominal pain, and shortness of breath. On examination she had abdominal tenderness with rebound. Her quantitative β-hCG level was 1011 mIU/mL. Transvaginal ultrasound showed free pelvic fluid, and liver enzymes were elevated (AST 193 U/L, ALT 270 U/L). Because of concern for a ruptured ectopic pregnancy, she underwent emergent laparotomy. At surgery there was 650 cc of hemoperitoneum, but both fallopian tubes appeared normal; the bleeding source was a ruptured subcapsular hepatic hematoma without active bleeding at that time.

Postoperatively, CT of the abdomen revealed multiple foci of active contrast extravasation in both hepatic lobes, concerning for hepatic arteriovenous malformations. A head CT angiogram showed a left parietal subarachnoid hemorrhage, and cerebral angiography identified a left parietal micro–arteriovenous malformation. Daily β-hCG levels over the next several days were 1011 → 748 → 861 → 1185 mIU/mL.
predicted_diagnoses: ['hepatic arteriovenous malformation', 'ruptured subcapsular hepatic hematoma', 'ectopic pregnancy']

case_text: A 30-year-old Chinese woman presented with a 6-year history of painless swelling in the left submandibular region that, over the last 3 months, had begun to extend to the root of the neck. On examination, there was an approximately 12 cm × 6 cm soft, fluctuant, non-tender mass in the left anterior cervical region, with no swelling visible in the floor of the mouth. Color Doppler ultrasound showed a well-defined, anechoic, loculated fluid collection in the left superficial anterior cervical region—extending from the mandibular angle to the suprasternal fossa—measuring about 10.98 cm × 1.49 cm × 4.92 cm, with poor internal sound transmission, visible soft-tissue separations, and no obvious blood-flow signal on color-flow Doppler imaging. Contrast-enhanced CT confirmed a non-enhancing cystic lesion extending from the submandibular region to the supraclavicular region. Fine-needle aspiration in clinic yielded an egg white–like liquid discharge.

An 18-year-old Iranian woman was admitted with a 2-week history of anorexia and yellow discoloration of the conjunctiva and urine. She had been taking tofacitinib, 5 mg daily, for treatment of alopecia areata for the past 5 months and reported no use of other medications, alcohol, or toxins.

On examination, she was icteric but hemodynamically stable. Initial laboratory tests showed:  
• Total bilirubin 16.4 mg/dL (normal 0.3–1), direct bilirubin 6.3 mg/dL (normal 0.1–0.3)  
• Serum transaminases elevated: SGOT 1179 IU/mL (normal 7–55), SGPT 960 IU/mL (normal 8–48)  
• Alkaline phosphatase 398 IU/mL (normal 40–129)  
• Normal blood cell counts  
• Prothrombin time 14.9 s (normal 11–13.5), INR 1.43 (normal 0.8–1.1)  

Three months after starting tofacitinib, routine labs showed SGOT 335 IU/mL, SGPT 414 IU/mL, and ALP 253 IU/mL. At the end of month 4, she stopped tofacitinib, and within days developed anorexia and icteric sclera. Repeat labs revealed SGOT 1239 IU/mL, SGPT 805 IU/mL, ALP 198 IU/mL, total bilirubin 8.42 mg/dL, direct bilirubin 7.15 mg/dL, PT 17.7 s, INR 1.34, and normal blood counts. She was diagnosed with drug hepatitis and hospitalized.

During the first week, liver dysfunction progressed, with INR rising toward acute liver failure. Abdominal ultrasonography showed a normal‐sized liver with reduced echogenicity, periportal echogenicity, and gallbladder wall thickening; the portal vein measured 8 mm with peak hepatorenal flow velocity of 15 cm/s. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis demonstrated a uniformly normal liver parenchyma without masses, normal bile ducts, and thickened gallbladder wall. Echocardiography was unremarkable. MRCP revealed normal intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts without stenosis, dilatation, or filling defects, with a contracted gallbladder and mild periportal edema.

A 40-year-old Japanese woman (gravida 2, para 2) presented with prolonged menorrhagia and severe anemia. Her history was notable for cervical carcinoma in situ treated with conization 10 years earlier, without recurrence. Six years before admission, transvaginal ultrasound identified a 3.5-cm uterine leiomyoma, which grew to 5 cm two years later. Over the last year, clinicians noted a 5-cm extrauterine mass protruding from the posterior uterine wall. On admission, MRI revealed a 10 × 9 × 7 cm pelvic mass in the left posterolateral myometrial wall with extrauterine extension and hemorrhagic cysts. Total abdominal hysterectomy with bilateral salpingectomy was performed for suspicion of uterine sarcoma or degenerated leiomyoma.

case_text:
A 47-year-old woman presented with a 2-month history of headache, vertigo, nausea, vomiting, right leg claudication, diffuse musculoskeletal pains, progressive bilateral hearing loss, and decreased vision progressing to near-blindness. On admission, her blood pressure was 125/75 mmHg in the left arm and 120/70 mmHg in the right arm; pulse 85/min; respiration rate 18/min; temperature 37.1°C. Heart and lung examinations were unremarkable.

Ophthalmologic examination showed visual acuity of 0.1 in both eyes and intraocular pressure of 15 mmHg bilaterally. Slit-lamp biomicroscopy revealed bilateral ciliary hyperemia, cataract, and interstitial keratitis with marginal corneal infiltrates. Funduscopic evaluation showed mild optic-disc hyperemia with blurred margins but no vitreous or retinal inflammation.

Audiologic testing, including pure-tone audiometry and auditory brainstem responses, demonstrated bilateral sensorineural hearing loss. Laboratory studies revealed a white blood cell count of 10,200/mm3, hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL, platelet count 340,000/mm3, erythrocyte sedimentation rate 65 mm/hr, and C-reactive protein 13.1 mg/dL.

Serologic tests were negative for autoimmune markers (anti-nuclear antibody, anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibody, rheumatoid factors, antiphospholipid antibodies, complements, and cryoglobulins) and for syphilis (VDRL, FTA-Abs). Cerebrospinal fluid and serum analyses were negative for antibodies (IgG and IgM) against Epstein-Barr virus, herpes zoster virus, herpes simplex virus, cytomegalovirus, chlamydia, hepatitis B and C viruses, HIV, Toxoplasma gondii, and Mycobacterium tuberculosis. Brain MRI, chest and abdominal CT, colonoscopy, upper endoscopy, and minor salivary gland biopsy were all normal.

A 54-year-old woman presented with a 5-day history of right upper eyelid swelling and temporal pain. Her ocular history included nasal pterygia and chronic dry eye. On examination, there was tenderness over the lacrimal gland and erythema on eversion of the right upper eyelid. An initial differential diagnosis included infectious or allergic periorbital edema. Her erythrocyte sedimentation rate was normal, and she was treated with oral azithromycin.

Three weeks later, her right periorbital edema had worsened despite antibiotics, and she had new-onset right lacrimal gland tenderness. Computed tomography of the orbits demonstrated right lacrimal gland inflammation and periorbital soft-tissue swelling. She was started on prednisone, 60 mg daily.

Two weeks after that, she had persistent symptoms and new-onset conjunctival injection of the right eye. The right conjunctiva blanched with topical phenylephrine. A diagnosis of dacryoadenitis and episcleritis was made, and she was prescribed clindamycin, 300 mg three times daily for 10 days, while continuing a prednisone taper.

Two months later, she returned with worsening periorbital edema and a sudden eruption of erythematous, smooth, indurated plaques on her right upper eyelid, right cheek, right chest, and right calf. A skin biopsy from one lesion showed a superficial and deep dermal perivascular lymphocytic infiltrate with increased dermal mucin.

Over the following week, the rash rapidly progressed to involve the chest, back, and upper extremities and became tender and pruritic. Repeat skin biopsies of cheek and calf lesions revealed vacuolar interface alterations with superficial and deep perivascular and perifollicular lymphocytic infiltrates and increased reticular dermal mucin. 

Laboratory studies—including complete blood count, metabolic panel, urinalysis, antinuclear antibody, anti–double‐stranded DNA, extractable nuclear antigen panel, complement levels (C3, C4), creatine kinase, aldolase, and rheumatoid factor—were all within normal limits.

A 4-year-old boy presented with 2 days of central, colicky abdominal pain, 4 days of vomiting, decreased oral intake, and fevers up to 38.5°C. His surgical history included an uncomplicated appendectomy. On examination, his temperature was 38.5°C, heart rate 105 BPM, blood pressure 111/66 mm Hg, SpO₂ 100%. He had tenderness and guarding in the left hypochondriac and left iliac regions. Laboratory studies were unremarkable. Abdominal ultrasound revealed a 3.2 cm mass in the left upper quadrant. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen showed a 3 × 2.5 cm cystic mass with thick enhancing walls in the mesenteric fat between colon and small bowel, homogeneous fluid content, and surrounding inflammatory fat stranding. A provisional diagnosis of abscess with an infected cyst or bowel duplication cyst was made.

A 52-year-old woman with hypertension presented with sudden episodes of falling to the ground without loss of consciousness over the past 5 months. Each episode lasted approximately 1 minute and was followed by full neurological recovery. She experienced 1–2 such events per day, often associated with giddiness. On examination, she had mild hyperreflexia in all four limbs but no other focal deficits.

Diagnostic studies included:
• Twenty-four-hour electroencephalography, which was normal.  
• Electromyography and nerve conduction studies of all four limbs, which were normal.  
• Cardiovascular evaluation, including electrocardiography and Holter monitoring, which were normal.

A 60-year-old Asian man with a 120–pack-year smoking history presented with intermittent dry cough, hemoptysis, chest tightness, and dyspnea for 3 months. He denied fever, weakness, or weight loss. Two months before admission, he received a 2-week course of antibiotics without improvement. On presentation he was afebrile (36.5 °C), blood pressure 105/60 mmHg, pulse 65 beats/min, respiratory rate 19 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 92 % on room air. Arterial blood gas analysis showed PaCO₂ 43 mmHg, PaO₂ 66 mmHg, and SaO₂ 92 %. Chest auscultation revealed decreased breath sounds and fine crackles bilaterally, more prominent in the left lower lung. He had no evidence of suspicious pigmented lesions on skin, mucosa, or eyes. 

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL, white blood cell count 10.46 × 10⁹/L (neutrophils 7.72 × 10⁹/L), and potassium 2.89 mmol/L. Metabolic panel was otherwise normal. Serum tumor markers (CEA, SCC, CYFRA21-1, NSE, AFP, CA19-9, CA72-4) were all normal. Tests for ANA, ANCA, thyroid function, ESR, T-SPOT, hepatitis, syphilis, and HIV were negative. Pulmonary function tests, including DLCO, were within normal limits. Fractional exhaled nitric oxide was 3.6 ppb.

Chest radiograph showed bilateral nodules with surrounding reticular fibrotic lesions. Contrast-enhanced CT of the chest revealed bilateral consolidations: a 28 × 24 mm nodule in the right upper lobe and a 33 × 43 mm subpleural mass in the left lower lobe, each surrounded by intralobular septal thickening and ground-glass opacity (“crazy-paving” pattern). Enlarged right hilar, mediastinal, and left axillary lymph nodes, bilateral pleural thickening, fibrotic changes, and small bilateral pleural effusions were also noted. A similar CT performed 2 months earlier demonstrated smaller nodules with the same surrounding paving pattern. 

He was treated empirically with cefoperazone/tazobactam and levofloxacin for presumed bacterial infection, without clinical or radiographic improvement.

A 74-year-old woman presented with a two-month history of daily left-sided abdominal cramps and pain. The cramps occurred throughout the day without identifiable triggers, were moderate initially, and later became intermittently severe. She reported one normal bowel movement daily but described a sensation of incomplete evacuation requiring laxatives. There was no fever, weight loss, or change in bowel habits. Her medical history included chronic renal disease, partial nephrectomy for renal cell carcinoma, hypercholesterolemia, gout, hypertension, and osteoporosis, all well controlled.  

On examination, vital signs were normal. Abdominal examination revealed mild to moderate tenderness in the left lower quadrant without palpable masses or organomegaly; bowel sounds were normal. Pelvic examination was unremarkable.  

Laboratory studies showed mild normocytic anemia and mildly elevated alanine aminotransferase; all other tests, including inflammatory markers, were within normal limits. Abdominal ultrasound confirmed chronic changes in the left kidney, a small simple cyst in the right kidney, and no intra-abdominal fluid or lymphadenopathy.  

Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis demonstrated a few sigmoid diverticula without signs of diverticulitis and a singular ill-defined 1.8×1.7 cm fat-density lesion with a peripheral soft tissue rim abutting the sigmoid colon. There was no intra-abdominal free fluid, lymphadenopathy, or other focal lesions. Stool occult blood was negative.

A 28-year-old man with acute myeloid leukemia refractory to two prior allogeneic hematopoietic stem-cell transplants was admitted for salvage chemotherapy. One year after his second transplant, he presented with recurrent pneumonia and developed watery diarrhea and a maculopapular skin rash. A CT scan on admission showed a left lower-lobe infiltrate but no intestinal edema; meropenem and vancomycin resolved his pulmonary symptoms. On hospital days 4–9, he received mitoxantrone, etoposide, and cytarabine. During chemotherapy, his diarrhea worsened and his rash spread. On day 10, abdominal CT revealed diffuse intestinal wall thickening. Laboratory studies showed white cells 20/μL, hemoglobin 8.4 g/dL, platelets 12 000/μL, lactate dehydrogenase 228 U/L, and C-reactive protein 1.24 mg/dL. Stool assays were negative for Clostridium difficile toxins A/B and glutamate dehydrogenase. Suspecting acute gut graft-versus-host disease, methylprednisolone was increased to 120 mg/day and beclomethasone dipropionate was started, but diarrhea continued to worsen. On days 13–15, he received methylprednisolone 1 g/day for 3 days. Colonoscopy was deferred because of his condition.

A 28-year-old man presented with progressive painless facial swelling over the last seven months and a change in his voice.  
His medical history was notable for end-stage renal disease of undetermined etiology for nine years, currently on hemodialysis three times per week, severe uncontrolled secondary hyperparathyroidism, and hypertension. He denied vision impairment, hearing loss, dyspnea, or dysphagia.  
On arrival, his blood pressure was 161/101 mmHg; other vital signs were normal. Physical examination revealed a hard, bony craniofacial protrusion most pronounced along the maxilla, without tenderness.  
Laboratory studies showed:  
• Urea 13.4 mmol/L (4.2–7.2)  
• Creatinine 920 µmol/L (64–115)  
• Estimated GFR 6 mL/min/1.73 m2  
• Phosphate 1.65 mmol/L (0.80–1.45)  
• Alkaline phosphatase 1714.3 U/L (50–116)  
• Calcium 2.080 mmol/L (2.100–2.550)  
• Parathyroid hormone 3012 ng/L (15–65)  
Noncontrast CT of the facial bones was performed:  
– Scout film demonstrated overgrowth of the jaws, particularly the maxilla, with multiple tiny lucencies in the calvaria (“salt-and-pepper” appearance).  
– Axial, coronal, and sagittal images showed significant jaw overgrowth with internal serpiginous tunneling or channeling and loss of corticomedullary differentiation.  
– Sagittal images revealed sclerosis of the vertebral endplates (“rugger-jersey spine”).

A 42-year-old Caucasian woman was admitted for fluctuating diplopia, ptosis, dysphagia, dysarthria, and fatigable chewing at the end of meals or at the end of the day. Her medical history included antiphospholipid syndrome, with three spontaneous abortions, and transient hypothyroidism. She took aspirin 81 mg daily. On examination, she had dysarthria and weakness of the facial muscles, tongue, and neck, and displayed progressive fatigability in upward gaze during the Simpson test. Repetitive ulnar nerve stimulation showed an abnormal (>10%) decrement. Tests for antibodies against AChR, MuSK, and titin were negative. A thoracic computed tomography scan was normal. Thromboplastin time and activated partial thromboplastin time were elevated; anticardiolipin and anti–β2-glycoprotein-I antibodies were elevated, consistent with her antiphospholipid syndrome.

An 81-year-old Japanese woman presented with chest pain at rest for one month. She had a history of papillary thyroid carcinoma treated surgically 30 years earlier and was followed for psychiatric problems and hypothyroidism. On admission, blood pressure was 125/81 mm Hg, heart rate 78 beats per minute, and oxygen saturation 95% on room air. Physical examination revealed no heart murmurs, jugular venous distention, or pedal edema. An electrocardiogram showed sinus rhythm with an incomplete right bundle branch block. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated a heterogeneous, irregular mass–like lesion measuring approximately 45×40 mm on the right ventricular free wall, contacting the tricuspid valve in diastole, with a small pericardial effusion. Transesophageal echocardiography showed part of the mass was mobile. Contrast-enhanced computed tomography revealed a 51 mm lobular mass involving the right ventricle and pericardium with heterogeneous enhancement. Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging showed the mass to be isointense on T1-weighted images and heterogeneously hyperintense on T2-weighted images. 18-Fluorodeoxyglucose positron emission tomography demonstrated increased uptake in the anterior lower wall of the right ventricle without evidence of metastasis. Coronary angiography showed normal coronary arteries and an arterial branch supplying the mass.
predicted_diagnoses: ['primary cardiac lymphoma', 'metastatic adenocarcinoma to the heart', 'hemangioma of the heart']

case_text: A 31-year-old woman with no prior psychiatric history was evaluated for a 21-month progression of neuropsychiatric symptoms. Initially she had a 2-week period of mood swings and compulsive behavior. Two months later, she had a first generalized seizure, affective lability, cognitive deficits, and aggression. CSF analysis showed WBC 23/µl and protein 487 mg/l; MRI of the brain was normal. She was treated empirically with ceftriaxone, aciclovir, clobazam, and valproate without clinical improvement. Over the next month she developed disorganized agitation, partial amnesia, disinhibition, delusions, and delirium, for which antipsychotics and benzodiazepines were used. At 3 months, she became catatonic with waxy flexibility and unresponsiveness to pain, and was diagnosed with catatonic schizophrenia. By 5 months, her catatonia resolved but she remained apathetic and slow. Nearly one year after onset she entered rehabilitation with persistent concentration problems and social withdrawal. Repeat CSF showed WBC 20/µl and protein 550 mg/l. Long-term EEG revealed left temporal and right hemispheric dysfunction without epileptiform discharges. Brain MRI showed right temporo-occipital signal changes. Antimicrobial therapy with aciclovir and piperacillin again failed. Antiepileptics were adjusted but poorly tolerated, and her condition worsened with enuresis and inability to care for herself. Twenty months after onset she was admitted to our psychiatry service. On exam she was tired but oriented; she had memory loss, impaired speech, intermittent mutism, echolalia, palilalia, right-hand fidgeting, left ocular muscle contractions, lip smacking, stereotypies, and waxy flexibility. CSF now showed protein 561 mg/l with an elevated albumin quotient, normal cell count, and no intrathecal Ig synthesis. EEG showed intermittent right-central delta foci and frontotemporal delta and theta components on independent component analysis. MRI revealed only moderate perisylvian/temporal atrophy. FDG-PET demonstrated global cortical hypometabolism of the left hemisphere, milder temporal hypometabolism on the right, and right cerebellar hypometabolism. Single-voxel 1H-MRS in the prefrontal cortices showed markedly reduced glutamate+glutamine concentrations and Glx/Cr ratios on the left compared with the right.

A 59-year-old man with benign prostatic hyperplasia treated with tamsulosin presented to the emergency department with a rash on his left nasolacrimal area and forehead, confined to the ophthalmic (V1) dermatome. Two days earlier, he had received the first dose of the recombinant zoster vaccine. He reported pain and tingling on the left side of his face but denied any vision changes or systemic symptoms. At an outside hospital on day 2 after vaccination, he had been prescribed amoxicillin, valacyclovir 1 g twice daily orally, and topical eyedrops. By day 9, the vesicles were fully developed and his symptoms had not improved, prompting this presentation. Examination revealed clustered vesicles and erythema limited to the left V1 dermatome; no other skin lesions or systemic signs were observed. Mupirocin 2% three times daily was prescribed to prevent secondary infection, and he was referred for ophthalmology consultation. During the ED visit, a sample was taken from the vesicles and sent for polymerase-chain-reaction testing for herpes simplex virus (HSV) 1 and 2 and varicella-zoster virus (VZV); an HIV antigen/antibody test was also ordered.
predicted_diagnoses: ['herpes zoster', 'herpes simplex virus (hsv) infection', 'varicella-zoster virus (vzv) infection']

case_text:
A 22-year-old man with a history of pan-ulcerative colitis presented with odynophagia and an inability to swallow due to significant oral ulceration and a 20–25 lb weight loss. He was diagnosed at age 20 and initially treated with prednisone and adalimumab; he remained asymptomatic for 8 months before discontinuing adalimumab. Two years later, he re-presented with one month of bloody diarrhea and weight loss during which adalimumab was re-induced along with a prednisone taper. His gastrointestinal symptoms resolved quickly; however, 7–10 days after re-induction, he developed severe oral mucosal ulcerations and a papular rash.

On admission, he was afebrile. Laboratory studies showed C-reactive protein 151.5 mg/L, white blood cell count 13.0×10^9/L with neutrophils 78.4%. Laryngoscopy demonstrated ulcerations along the epiglottis. On hospital day 2, he developed a fever to 38.3 °C and was noted to have erythematous, scaly papules and nodules on his face, scalp, trunk, and buttocks draining a transparent yellow discharge. Dermatology punch biopsies were obtained from both skin lesions and oral ulcers. The gingival biopsies were nonspecific; the skin biopsy showed a dense neutrophilic infiltrate in the dermis with microabscess formation in the epidermis and superficial dermis.

A 30-year-old man presented with 3 months of progressive right upper back pain and severe episodes of nonproductive cough. He had no fever, dyspnea on exertion, nausea, or vomiting. His medical history was notable for a motorcycle accident 12 years earlier, with thoracic vertebral fracture repaired via an anterior approach, without complications and with full recovery.

On examination, vital signs were normal. Mild tenderness was noted on palpation of the right upper back. Auscultation of the right thorax revealed small clicking, bubbling, and rattling sounds. Chest radiography showed a 6×3 cm opacity with several mottled small air densities in the right lung field. Contrast-enhanced CT of the thorax demonstrated an 8×5×8 cm low-density heterogeneous mass in the right posterior mediastinum, with a spongiform pattern containing air bubbles and well-defined borders; no lymphadenopathy or other masses were identified.

A 32-year-old woman presented with 15 days of fever, fatigue, and chest and abdominal pain. She had no personal or family history of tuberculosis or malignancy. Her temperature was 39°C, blood pressure 140/80 mmHg, heart rate 88 bpm, and respiratory rate 16 breaths/min. On examination, she was lethargic and had a maculopapular rash on her face, trunk, and extremities. She had multiple, discrete, freely mobile, non-tender lymph nodes in the cervical, axillary, and inguinal regions, each measuring 0.5–3.7 cm. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations were normal.

Laboratory tests showed hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL; leukocytes 17 800/mm³ (eosinophils 9.8%, neutrophils 54.3%, monocytes 14%); platelets 205 000/mm³; lactate dehydrogenase 578 U/L; C-reactive protein 28.2 mg/L; and erythrocyte sedimentation rate 77 mm/hour. Liver and renal function tests were normal. Serologic tests for toxoplasma, Epstein–Barr virus, rubella, cytomegalovirus, herpes simplex type 1, hepatitis B, and hepatitis C were all negative. Blood, urine, and sputum cultures, including cultures for acid-fast bacilli, were negative. Protein electrophoresis showed polyclonal gammopathy. Antinuclear antibody, anti–double-stranded DNA antibody, p-ANCA, c-ANCA, extractable nuclear antigens, and rheumatoid factor were all negative. Electrocardiogram and chest radiograph were unremarkable.

Computed tomography of the neck demonstrated lymphadenomegaly of various sizes. Further computed tomography showed generalized lymphadenopathy in the cervical, axillary, paraaortic, mediastinal, and inguinal regions. A cervical lymph-node biopsy was performed.

A 17-year-old nulliparous unmarried girl was brought to the gynecology ward after collapsing at home. She complained of a mass protruding through the vagina following a Valsalva maneuver and persistent lower abdominal pain, without vaginal bleeding. Four days earlier she had attempted an induced abortion at 18 weeks’ gestation with misoprostol; products of conception were not expelled, and she underwent surgical uterine evacuation at another clinic the next day. On admission, she was agitated, severely pale, and cold (temperature 35.8 °C), in shock with a feeble radial pulse of 143 beats/min and unrecordable blood pressure. The abdomen was soft and nontender with no palpable masses. Vaginal inspection revealed a fleshy mass approximately 8 × 5 cm with gangrenous patches protruding at the introitus; the surface was not actively bleeding but foul-smelling pus oozed from necrotic areas. On digital vaginal examination, the cervical lip was not felt around the mass, and bimanual palpation revealed absence of the uterine fundus at its expected periumbilical position with a cupping sensation. Initial hemoglobin was 4.5 g/dL and leukocyte count was 12,600/µL (89% neutrophils).

A 32-year-old woman presented with 12 hours of nausea, vomiting, dizziness, and epigastric pain. Earlier that day, after an argument, she took eight Tylenol tablets (total 2.6 g of acetaminophen). She denied alcohol use, gallbladder stones, other drug ingestion, trauma, autoimmune disease, and diabetes. On examination, vital signs were within normal limits. The upper abdomen was soft with mild tenderness; there was no jaundice, hepatosplenomegaly, ascites, or Murphy’s sign. Initial laboratory tests showed hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL, white cells 5.5 × 10^9/L, neutrophils 65%, platelets 169 × 10^9/L; AST 13 U/L (7–40), ALT 13 U/L (13–35), albumin 3.3 g/dL; serum amylase 990 U/L (0–96), lipase 49.5 U/L (13–60), triglycerides 0.6 mmol/L (0.34–1.70). Two days later, aminotransferases remained normal, amylase 433 U/L, lipase 136.6 U/L, triglycerides 0.6 mmol/L, creatinine 53 μmol/L (45–84). Viral hepatitis markers (A, B, C, D, E), antinuclear antibody, p-ANCA, c-ANCA, and IgG4 were all within normal limits or negative. Abdominal ultrasonography was unremarkable. Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography showed a bulky pancreas without peripancreatic exudation. The patient received standard pancreatitis care and N-acetylcysteine.

A 33-year-old gravida 1 woman conceived with letrozole ovulation induction and intrauterine insemination. Her medical history was notable for migraines; she had undergone laparoscopic right salpingo-oophorectomy and appendectomy for chronic right-sided pelvic pain. 

At 5 weeks 2 days’ gestation, transvaginal ultrasound showed three gestational sacs, only two of which contained yolk sacs. At 6 weeks 2 days, ultrasound confirmed a viable dichorionic twin pregnancy with two gestational sacs, two yolk sacs, and two fetal poles. 

At 9 weeks 2 days, she experienced vaginal bleeding and again 4 days later. In both emergency evaluations, ultrasound confirmed a viable twin pregnancy with a normal dividing membrane and no subchorionic hemorrhage; no intertwin hemorrhage was seen. She continued to have intermittent bleeding but remained hemodynamically stable. 

At 11 weeks, her vaginal bleeding had ceased; she denied contractions, cramping, and discharge. Transvaginal ultrasound demonstrated a 15.0 mm thickness of the intertwin dividing membrane, consistent with an intertwin membrane hemorrhage. Baby aspirin was held, and she was counseled on bleeding and preterm labor precautions.

A 36-year-old Japanese man with biopsy-proven sarcoidosis was referred for ocular evaluation in July 2017. Two days earlier, he had begun oral prednisolone (70 mg daily) for thrombocytopenic purpura (platelet count, 4000/μL) attributed to sarcoidosis-related splenomegaly. His history included cervical lymph node, subcutaneous nodule, and transbronchial lung biopsies (2015–2016), all showing noncaseating granulomas. Serum angiotensin-converting enzyme was 8.4–16.4 IU/L (normal); serum lysozyme was elevated. Tests for toxoplasma, tuberculosis, and other infections were negative; serum calcium, eosinophil count, and ESR were normal.

On examination, best-corrected visual acuity was 20/20 and intraocular pressures were normal in both eyes. There were no signs of intraocular inflammation. Fundus examination of the left eye revealed an asymptomatic, white, round choroidal mass (1.5 disc diameters) superior-temporal to the macula. B-scan ultrasonography showed an intrachoroidal lesion without calcification. Enhanced depth imaging–OCT demonstrated a dome-shaped choroidal elevation with effacement of choroidal vessels and overlying subretinal fluid. Fluorescein angiography showed early hypofluorescence with late leakage; indocyanine green angiography showed persistent hypofluorescence. Brain/orbit MRI and CT scans showed no orbital mass or neurosarcoidosis; whole-body PET-CT found no evidence of malignancy.

A 58-year-old man with no known chronic illness was brought to the emergency department with one week of fatigue and progressive dyspnea. On arrival, he was tachypneic and hypotensive, and preliminary laboratories revealed: hemoglobin 21 g/L, mean corpuscular volume 154 fL, indeterminate platelet count, creatinine 219 μmol/L, and venous blood gas pH 7.19. He was intubated for respiratory distress and started on renal replacement therapy for acute kidney injury.  

His only medications were amlodipine and ezetimibe. He denied fevers, diarrheal illness, melena, or sick contacts. Physical examination showed jaundice but no petechiae, and a digital rectal examination was negative for blood.  

Initial investigations revealed:  
• A significant degree of red blood cell agglutination on peripheral blood film  
• A positive direct antiglobulin (Coombs) test for C3  
• Haptoglobin < 0.07 g/L  
• Indirect bilirubin 83.1 μmol/L  
• Lactate dehydrogenase 1776 U/L (normal < 225 U/L)  
• A cold agglutinin screen positive at a thermal amplitude of 37 °C

A 10-year-old otherwise healthy prepubertal girl was referred for evaluation of a dark spot in her right eye, first noted 3 years earlier and recently noted to be enlarging. She had no systemic complaints and was on no chronic medications. On examination, visual acuity was 20/20 OU and intraocular pressures were 17 mm Hg bilaterally. Slit-lamp examination of the left eye and dilated fundus examination OU were normal. In the right eye, there was a well-demarcated, minimally elevated, irregularly pigmented brown-black conjunctival lesion measuring 1.2 × 1.4 mm, mobile over the sclera, encroaching the limbus without corneal involvement and without visible intralesional cysts. AS-OCT through the lesion demonstrated a homogenous deep conjunctival stromal mass with intense hyperreflectivity and posterior shadowing, intact overlying epithelium, and no scleral invasion. The working differential diagnosis included primary acquired melanosis, atypical conjunctival nevus, and, less likely, malignant conjunctival melanoma. Observation was elected. 
At 6 months, the lesion measured 1.1 × 1.5 mm with a new focus of darker episcleral pigmentation; AS-OCT showed corresponding hyperreflectivity and back shadowing. Observation continued.  
At 1 year, the lesion exhibited further radial elongation of dark pigmentation reaching the peripheral cornea, with 0.5 mm of subepithelial corneal pigmentation; AS-OCT confirmed deeper stromal involvement with focal hyperreflectivity and shadowing and still no cysts. Given progression, corneal involvement, and absence of cysts, the decision was made to perform an excisional corneal, conjunctival, and partial lamellar scleral biopsy.
predicted_diagnoses: ['malignant conjunctival melanoma', 'atypical conjunctival nevus', 'primary acquired melanosis']

case_text:
A 55-year-old man with diabetes, hypertension, paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, and chronic kidney disease presented with painless frank hematuria for 2 months. Cystoscopy revealed a 5 cm posterior bladder wall tumor; biopsy showed infiltrating high-grade urothelial carcinoma, pT2. He underwent transurethral resection and contrast-enhanced CT of the thorax, abdomen, and pelvis, which showed no metastatic disease. After a prolonged postoperative hospitalization with multiple complications, serial CT scans remained negative. Seven months later, he reported lower back pain and was found to have elevated serum calcium. Bony metastasis was suspected, but repeat CT scans again showed no local recurrence or distant metastasis. Two months thereafter, he developed left knee pain and swelling. 
Plain radiography of the left tibia–fibula demonstrated a lytic destructive lesion in the proximal fibula. Ultrasound of the left calf showed a 2.0 × 3.2 × 3.9 cm oval heterogeneous intramuscular hypoechoic lesion in the proximal lateral gastrocnemius muscle with peripheral vascularity. MRI of the left knee revealed an ill-defined enhancing soft tissue mass in the proximal lateral gastrocnemius muscle that was isointense on T1-weighted images, heterogeneously hyperintense on T2-weighted images, measured 2.5 × 4.3 × 4.2 cm, and was associated with surrounding muscle edema and adjacent fibular marrow hyperintensity with postcontrast enhancement.

A 58-year-old man was referred to the ENT outpatient clinic on an urgent two-week-wait pathway with a history of intermittent exertional stridor, dysphonia, and dysphagia. His symptoms had recurred over multiple emergency department visits, and an upper gastrointestinal endoscopy was normal. He had undergone a partial thyroidectomy for multinodular goitre in 2006 and a revision right hemithyroidectomy in 2017 for compressive symptoms. He was prescribed thyroxine and calcium supplements but admitted to poor adherence with calcium.

On examination, there were well-healed thyroidectomy scars. Flexible nasendoscopy revealed tongue-base fullness with symmetrical vocal-cord mobility and no mucosal lesions. A CT scan of the neck and thorax showed a small mucous-retention cyst at the tongue base and residual thyroid tissue bilaterally but no evidence of malignancy.

He was scheduled for panendoscopy under general anaesthesia, and routine pre-operative blood tests were obtained.

case_text: A 76-year-old Caucasian man in Brazil with stage IIIC superficial spreading melanoma of the trunk (Breslow thickness 5.0 mm, ulcerated, pT4b pN1a M0) underwent wide excision and sentinel-node biopsy showing micrometastasis. He received adjuvant nivolumab (3 mg/kg every 14 days) for 12 months without significant adverse events. Two months after completing therapy, he developed weakness, anorexia, daily fevers to 38.0 °C, night sweats, weight loss, diffuse petechiae, and splenomegaly. He denied recent travel, new medications, and sick contacts. Laboratory studies revealed hemoglobin 8.5 g/dL, platelets 68 × 10^3/mL, fibrinogen 138 mg/dL, and ferritin 20 406 µg/L. ^18F-FDG PET-CT showed intense uptake in the spleen and in cervical, supraclavicular, mediastinal, and abdominal lymph nodes, with an SUVmax of 14.8 in an infracarinal node. Bone marrow aspiration was hypercellular without other abnormalities. He met five of eight criteria for HLH and was treated with dexamethasone and intravenous immunoglobulin. Extensive laboratory tests and cultures failed to identify an infectious source. A percutaneous core biopsy of the hypermetabolic infracarinal lymph node was then performed.

A 24-day-old term male infant presented with a 48-hour history of non-bilious projectile vomiting. He was born at 39 weeks’ gestation via uncomplicated vaginal delivery, with birth weight 3050 g, length 47 cm, and head circumference 35.5 cm. Prenatal sonograms were normal. On examination, the infant appeared well, with no skin lesions and no palpable organomegaly. 

An abdominal ultrasound demonstrated pyloric muscle thickness of 4 mm and pyloric canal length of 25 mm, consistent with hypertrophic pyloric stenosis. Incidentally, the liver was enlarged and had a heterogeneous appearance with multiple hypoechoic nodules in both lobes. 

Laboratory tests, including complete blood count, electrolytes, liver function tests, thyroid function tests, alpha-fetoprotein, vanillylmandelic acid, and neuron specific enolase, were all within normal limits. 

Abdominal magnetic resonance imaging showed diffuse hepatomegaly with innumerable nodules throughout both lobes; the lesions were hyperintense on T2-weighted images with prominent flow voids and hypointense on T1-weighted images. 

A transthoracic echocardiogram showed normal cardiac anatomy and function, with no evidence of high-output heart failure.

A 39-year-old woman presented with a two-month history of progressive foreign-body sensation in her throat and discomfort with swallowing. She first noticed a painless mass on the right side of her soft palate, which had gradually increased in size. She was otherwise healthy, with no history of smoking, alcohol use, or familial tumor syndromes. On examination, there was a solitary, cystic, non-tender soft tissue mass protruding from the right side of the soft palate; no cervical lymphadenopathy was noted. Laboratory studies were within normal limits. Contrast-enhanced CT of the head and neck demonstrated a well-defined, non-enhancing, homogeneous, pedunculated soft tissue lesion measuring 2.1 × 1.6 × 1.5 cm arising from the posterior edge of the right soft palate, with smooth margins and no signs of invasion.

A 23-year-old man presented with a 1-week history of progressive shortness of breath and a 1-month history of right-sided chest pain, dry cough, and abdominal cramps. He denied fever and night sweats. One month earlier, he had been hospitalized for chest pain. On examination, he appeared moderately ill. Blood pressure was 130/70 mmHg, heart rate 108 beats/min, respiratory rate 28 breaths/min, temperature 36.8 °C, and oxygen saturation 94 % on room air (98 % on 3 L nasal cannula). The anterior chest showed symmetrical movement and diminished breath sounds in the right lower lung. On posterior chest examination, there was a palpable lump in the right postero-inferior chest wall. Laboratory testing revealed thrombocytosis and an elevated D-dimer level. Chest radiography showed a mass in the right lower posterior lung field with moderate right pleural effusion. Thoracic ultrasonography demonstrated a right-sided lung mass partially adherent to the inner chest wall and moderate pleural effusion. Contrast-enhanced CT of the thorax revealed a lobulated soft-tissue mass measuring approximately 7.7 × 7 cm along the right posterolateral thoracic wall or pleura, with an expansile component involving the right 11th rib. MRI of the thorax showed a 14 × 11 × 10 cm necrotic mass in the posterior right lower thoracic wall, infiltrating soft tissue, eroding posterior ribs 8 and destroying ribs 9 and 10, and extending into the thoracic cavity; there were also lesions in the left lateral ribs 3–5 and atelectasis of the right lower lobe. The working imaging differential was a soft-tissue sarcoma versus a malignant bone tumor. The patient subsequently underwent transthoracic needle aspiration and core biopsy.

case_text:
A 51-year-old woman with a history of right shoulder melanoma excised seven years earlier presented with chest pain and palpitations. Emergency medical services recorded a wide-complex tachycardia that terminated after vagal maneuvers. On admission, her vital signs and physical examination were normal. Laboratory studies showed a peak high-sensitivity troponin I of 935 ng/L (normal < 49 ng/L), with normal blood counts and metabolic panel. A chest radiograph was unremarkable. The arrhythmia was diagnosed as monomorphic ventricular tachycardia based on right bundle-branch–block morphology not present in sinus rhythm, a monophasic R wave in V1, and an R:S ratio < 1 in V6. Coronary angiography revealed normal coronary arteries. Cardiac magnetic resonance (CMR) imaging was performed to evaluate for scar or infiltration and demonstrated a left ventricular mass, prompting transfer for further evaluation.

Transthoracic echocardiography with contrast showed a small LV chamber without outflow or intracavitary obstruction, mild mitral regurgitation, and a non-mobile mass in the mid-to-basal septum and inferior walls with mild contrast enhancement. There was normal biventricular systolic function and no regional wall-motion abnormalities.

Outside-hospital CMR (1.5 T) confirmed a 5.9 × 4.4 × 2.6 cm mass in the mid-to-basal inferoseptal and inferior walls with eccentric mitral regurgitation due to restricted posterior leaflet motion. On CMR:
• T2-weighted fat-suppressed images showed hyperintense signal in the mass compared with myocardium.  
• T1-weighted images showed hyperintense signal in the mass relative to myocardium.  
• Inversion-recovery steady-state images (IR 600 ms) showed the mass to be isointense to myocardium.  
• T1 mapping demonstrated elevated T1 relaxation times in the mass.  
• First-pass perfusion imaging showed significant perfusion of the mass.  
• Delayed myocardial enhancement sequences showed diffuse enhancement throughout the mass.

Contrast-enhanced chest, abdomen, and pelvis CT did not identify any extracardiac primary malignancy. The mass lacked calcification and measured 74 Hounsfield units, inconsistent with fat or fluid.

On the basis of imaging, the differential included secondary metastatic tumour or a primary malignant tumour such as angiosarcoma or rhabdomyosarcoma, while benign primary tumours (haemangioma, myxoma) were deemed less likely.

A 56-year-old woman presented with diffuse abdominal pain of 3 weeks’ duration. She denied nausea or vomiting and reported normal passage of stool and gas. On examination, she was hemodynamically stable. Abdominal palpation elicited diffuse pain with tenderness, most pronounced in the upper quadrants; peristalsis was reduced, tympanism was normal, and Blumberg sign was positive. Laboratory studies showed elevated C-reactive protein, fibrinogen, and γ-glutamyltransferase, and a significant drop in hemoglobin over 24–36 hours. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen revealed wall thickening of the right colon and proximal transverse colon, stretching of the mesentery and adjacent vessels, surrounding lymphadenopathy, and a characteristic “target” appearance. Within the lumen was a round 44×43 mm mass of mixed density, predominantly adipose in attenuation.

A 43-year-old woman woke with left-sided neck discomfort 10 days before presentation. The pain radiated to the trapezius and scapular region and worsened with neck movement. Four days after the pain began, she noted difficulty abducting her left arm. She denied sensory changes, headache, or other neurologic symptoms. She recalled mild cervical discomfort when rotating her head while reversing her car about 4 days before the onset of weakness.

On examination, strength was 4/5 in the left deltoid, supraspinatus, infraspinatus, and biceps muscles, with 4+/5 strength in forearm pronation and supination. The left biceps reflex was absent, and the left brachioradialis reflex was reduced. Sensation in the left upper limb was intact, and cranial nerve examination was normal. The remainder of the neurologic examination was unremarkable.

Cervical magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and MR angiography (MRA) were performed to evaluate the cause of her symptoms.

A 70-year-old man was referred for evaluation of a painful ulcer on the right lateral border of the tongue present for two years. He described dull, continuous pain, a burning sensation with spicy foods, difficulty chewing and swallowing, restricted tongue movements, weight loss, loss of appetite, decreased salivation, and hoarseness of voice. His habits included kharra (smokeless tobacco) chewing two to three times daily for two years and a 20–25–year history of bidi smoking (two to three per day), which he had quit 10 years earlier. 

On extraoral examination, bilateral tender submandibular lymph nodes measuring approximately 3 × 4 cm were palpable. Intraoral examination revealed a 2 × 3 cm ulceroproliferative lesion on the right lateral tongue border extending from the dorsal to ventral surfaces, from the lower right molar region to the retromolar trigone and involving the soft palate. The lesion had everted margins and was indurated on palpation. A provisional diagnosis of tongue malignancy was made.

Contrast-enhanced MRI of the tongue showed a 7.6 × 4.5 × 4.3 cm heterogeneously enhancing mass on the right side of the tongue with areas of necrosis, abutting the lingual septum medially and extending into the infratemporal fossa laterally. Multiple subcentimetric to centimetric heterogeneously enhancing lymph nodes were noted in the submental, bilateral submandibular, and jugulodigastric regions; the largest measured 4.3 × 3.2 cm in the right submandibular region with necrotic areas. The radiologic impression was tongue carcinoma with lymphadenopathy.

An incisional biopsy of the tongue lesion was performed.
predicted_diagnoses: ['squamous cell carcinoma of the tongue', 'lymphoma', 'metastatic carcinoma']
