case_text:
A 79-year-old man living in a rural area was admitted for acute right upper-limb weakness and fever. Ten days earlier he had developed fever (39 °C), treated at home with acetaminophen and antibiotics. He had no trauma or recent travel. Medical history included type II diabetes, hypertension, and hypothyroidism. 

On admission: temperature 37.8 °C, respiratory rate 30/min, blood pressure 190/100 mmHg, signs of dehydration. Neurologic exam showed right upper-limb muscle strength of 2/5 in the triceps, wrist and finger extensors and flexors, and 3/5 in the biceps, arm abductors, arm flexors, and forearm; no altered mental status, pain, sensory changes, or meningeal signs. 

Laboratory tests: normal inflammatory markers; blood and urine cultures negative. Brain CT and MRI were unremarkable. Chest CT and abdominal ultrasound showed no pathology. On further history, he reported multiple mosquito bites. Serum IgM, IgG, and RT-PCR for West Nile virus were positive, meeting CDC criteria for infection. 

After spontaneous defervescence, on hospital day 4 he developed acute right upper-limb pain that worsened at night and was unchanged by rest or activity. Neurologic re-examination showed new weakness (2/5) of interossei and thenar and hypothenar muscles and 3/5 strength in the trapezius; sensory testing was not repeated. 

Shoulder ultrasound demonstrated supra- and infraspinatus tendinosis with calcifications and full-thickness rupture of the subscapularis tendon. Cervical spine MRI showed a right posterolateral C7–C8 disc protrusion. Nerve conduction studies and electromyography revealed (1) symmetric mixed sensorimotor polyneuropathy, mainly in the lower limbs; (2) cervical polyradiculopathy predominant at right C7–C8; and (3) signs of recent nerve damage at right C5–C7 with preservation of sensory nerve potentials.

A 47-year-old woman with well-controlled diabetes mellitus, dyslipidemia, anemia, hypothyroidism, and myopia presented with right ocular pain and erythema after a knitting-needle injury. On initial examination, right-eye visual acuity was hand motion and left eye was 8/10. Pupils were equal and reactive bilaterally, with a 1+ relative afferent pupillary defect on the right. Slit-lamp examination of the right eye revealed a partial-thickness scleral laceration, conjunctival laceration, and hyphema; the left anterior chamber was deep and clear. Intraocular pressure was 10 mm Hg in both eyes, extraocular movements were normal, and fundus examination of the right eye was obscured by hyphema. The lacerations were repaired, and on the following day the patient reported new-onset blurred vision. Examination then revealed hyperemic conjunctiva, subconjunctival hemorrhage, visible suture tracks, 4+ red blood cells in the anterior chamber, early cataract changes, and intraocular pressure of 14 mm Hg in both eyes. Posterior-segment examination of the right eye showed a diminished foveal reflex, retinal edema at the posterior pole, and a cherry-red spot; visual acuity remained hand motion at 1 m with a 1+ relative afferent pupillary defect. Optical coherence tomography of the right eye revealed marked hyperreflectivity and thickening of the inner retinal layers, with loss of their normal stratification.
predicted_diagnoses: ['central retinal artery occlusion', 'acute angle-closure glaucoma', 'optic neuritis']

case_text:
A 35-year-old man presented with 18 months of gradually progressive difficulty walking, characterized by dragging of the right foot and slippage of footwear, without significant interference in daily activities. Six months after onset, he developed spontaneous painful drawing up of both legs in response to noxious or non-noxious stimuli; these movements became frequent over the past two months and disturbed his sleep. He also noted urinary frequency, urgency, and incontinence for one year. He denied sensory disturbances, headaches, vomiting, seizures, trauma, or fever. He had intermittent low backache for two years, radiating along the posterior right thigh, responsive to analgesics.

On examination, he was of average build with normal vital signs and no cranial nerve or higher cortical deficits. Motor examination of the lower limbs showed grade 3 spasticity on the right and grade 2 on the left; right ankle dorsiflexors, evertors, and invertors were 0/5, plantarflexors 5/5; power was normal elsewhere. Flexor spasms were present bilaterally (spasm score 4). Deep tendon reflexes were brisk at knees and ankles bilaterally; the right plantar response was extensor, the left plantar was not elicitable. Sensory testing revealed a 1-cm decrease in two-point discrimination on the anterior right leg compared with the left, with intact primary modalities and cortical sensations. Gait showed a right high steppage pattern.

Laboratory studies, including complete blood count and serum biochemistry, were normal. MR imaging of the cervical and thoracic spine was unremarkable. Lumbar MRI revealed a disc prolapse at L4–L5 with minimal thecal sac compression. The presence of an abnormal cortical sensory finding despite normal primary modalities prompted consideration of an intracranial lesion.

A 32-year-old man with hypertension, recurrent intravenous drug use, and end-stage renal disease on thrice-weekly haemodialysis via a left upper-extremity arterio-venous graft presented with 2 weeks of subjective fevers, chills, productive cough with whitish sputum, right-sided pleuritic chest pain, and rhinorrhea. He was febrile (39.2°C), tachycardic (110 bpm), and had a diastolic murmur best heard at the left upper sternal border. The dialysis graft site appeared healthy. Six months earlier, he had been diagnosed with native pulmonic valve infective endocarditis due to Staphylococcus epidermidis and had received intravenous vancomycin, gentamicin, and rifampin, but left care against medical advice before sensitivities were available.

Laboratory studies showed a leukocyte count of 13,800/mm3, C-reactive protein 219 mg/L, procalcitonin 39.5 ng/dL, and lactate 2.2 mmol/L. A chest radiograph demonstrated a small left pleural effusion. A provisional diagnosis of community-acquired pneumonia with sepsis was made, and he was started on vancomycin and piperacillin–tazobactam.

Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a 0.5 × 0.6 cm sessile, echogenic vegetation on the arterial side of the pulmonic valve with severe pulmonic regurgitation. Chest computed tomography showed multiple bilateral cavitary nodules consistent with septic emboli and ground-glass opacities. Blood cultures grew methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus.
predicted_diagnoses: ['staphylococcus aureus infective endocarditis', 'pulmonary septic embolism', 'community-acquired pneumonia']

case_text:
A 39-year-old woman with no prior medical history presented repeatedly with brief episodes of aphasia, dysarthria, and tingling in her right arm. She was a never-smoker, had no known cardiovascular risk factors, and denied epistaxis, hemoptysis, or exertional dyspnea. Physical examination showed no platypnea-orthodeoxia, clubbing, or mucocutaneous telangiectasia. Arterial blood gas on room air revealed a pO2 of 10.6 kPa. Hemoglobin was normal, and there was no polycythemia. An electrocardiogram showed normal sinus rhythm without arrhythmia. Brain MRI demonstrated lesions compatible with old ischemia. She was diagnosed with recurrent transient ischemic attacks.  
Given her age, lack of risk factors, and recurrence of events, a bubble-contrast transesophageal echocardiogram was performed; more than 30 bubbles appeared in the left atrium after six cardiac cycles, indicating a right-to-left shunt. A patent foramen ovale was thought to be the source, and the patient was scheduled for PFO closure. However, during the procedure no PFO could be identified, and an intracardiac shunt was excluded.

A 55‐year‐old Indian man presented with 1½ months of progressive lower abdominal distension and 15 days of continuous, dull aching pain. He denied trauma, weight loss, anorexia, altered bowel habits, fever, vomiting, or urinary symptoms. He was a chronic smoker. On examination, he was moderately built, with visible fullness in the left lower abdomen. Palpation revealed a smooth, ill‐defined, non‐tender mass approximately 20×15 cm, extending from the left iliac and hypogastric regions across midline to the right midclavicular line at the iliac crest. The mass was firm‐cystic with restricted mobility. There was no inguinal lymphadenopathy and digital rectal examination was unremarkable.  
Ultrasonography showed a large septated cystic lesion left of the urinary bladder, with the liver, spleen, pancreas, and kidneys appearing normal. Contrast‐enhanced CT demonstrated a 14.4×14.6×9.2 cm multilocular cystic mass with enhancing septations occupying the left abdomen and pelvis, displacing gut loops and bladder to the right, and encasing the left iliac artery; attenuation was +10 to +15 HU, with no ascites or bowel obstruction.  
Fine‐needle aspiration of the mass yielded red blood cells, neutrophils, and lymphocytes in a proteinaceous background, suggesting a lymphangiomatous process. A provisional diagnosis of retroperitoneal lymphangioma versus mesenteric cyst was made.

A 27-year-old man presented with a 2-year history of a gradually enlarging, asymptomatic swelling of the lower left jaw and intermittent pus discharge from the lower left posterior teeth region. Ten to twelve years earlier, he had undergone extraction of a lower left molar, leaving an unhealed socket that had been unsuccessfully treated by multiple practitioners. The patient was otherwise healthy and alert. 

On extraoral examination, there was a 7 × 5 cm ill-defined, noncompressible, nonfluctuant swelling over the left angle of the mandible, fixed to underlying structures; a soft, fluctuant notch was noted in the body of the mandible 3 cm anterior to the angle, and the jaw deviated toward the right on opening. Intraorally, there was buccal cortical expansion from tooth 35 to the angle, egg-shell crackling near tooth 37, lingual plate expansion at teeth 37–38, and yellow discharge from the socket of tooth 38 and the root stump of tooth 36. Tooth 37 was nonvital on pulp testing. 

A provisional clinical diagnosis was ameloblastoma of the left mandible, with a differential that included residual cyst, dentigerous cyst, keratocystic odontogenic tumour, central giant cell granuloma, and radicular cyst. Intraoral periapical radiography showed a radiolucency extending beyond the film margins. A panoramic radiograph revealed a multilocular radiolucency with scalloped, hyperostotic borders, extending from the posterior body of the mandible through the angle, ramus, coronoid process, and condyle, with downward expansion of the inferior mandibular border, a 3 × 2.5 cm radiolucent “window” below tooth 37, and resorption of the mesial and distal roots of tooth 37. 

CT imaging confirmed involvement of the coronoid process, condyle, ramus, and body of the left mandible, and demonstrated medial deflection of the left lateral pterygoid plate and of the left maxillary posterior alveolar arch.

A 48-year-old man, with obesity and moderate alcohol consumption but no smoking history, first presented at age 41 with two months of progressive abdominal distension and dyspnea. Physical examination revealed diffuse edema—most prominent in the abdomen—and diminished breath sounds with dullness to percussion. Laboratory studies showed CRP 6.0 mg/dL (normal < 0.3 mg/dL), mild liver cytolysis, hypoalbuminemia (albumin 2.3 g/dL), and a normal 24-hour urinalysis. Serologic and molecular testing for Bartonella spp., Coxiella burnetii, Brucella spp., Borrelia spp., Chlamydia spp., and Mycobacterium tuberculosis were negative. Immunology studies revealed an ANA titer of 1:320 and an anti-SS-A/Ro titer of 98 U/mL (reference < 7 U/mL); anti-dsDNA, ANCA, rheumatoid factor, and anti-CCP were negative; complement levels were normal. Pleural and ascitic fluid analyses demonstrated transudative effusions without malignant cells. A pleural biopsy showed nonspecific reactive inflammatory changes. Schirmer test was abnormal (3 mm in 5 min bilaterally) but minor salivary gland biopsy did not show sialadenitis. He fulfilled 2016 ACR/EULAR criteria for primary Sjögren’s syndrome and was treated with prednisolone 60 mg/day. The patient remained stable for two years, then was readmitted with dyspnea and abdominal distension; thoracic CT showed localized pericardial thickening and a thin circumferential pericardial effusion. Diuretics were adjusted. Four years after initial presentation, he returned with relapsed dyspnea, orthopnea, and ascites. Examination revealed jugular venous distension, crackles at lung bases, ascites, and bilateral leg edema. Chest X-ray showed a large right pleural effusion with passive atelectasis. Electrocardiogram demonstrated atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 86 bpm and diffuse T-wave flattening.

An 87-year-old woman presented with a lesion involving her left cheek, nasal ala, medial canthal area, and eyelids. She had no known precipitating factors and an unremarkable medical history.

On examination, there was an irregular cutaneous lesion extending across the left cheek, nasal skin, medial canthus, and eyelids. Two small, non-orientable grayish skin biopsies (0.8 cm and 0.6 cm) were obtained.

Histologic examination of 16 levels revealed a malignant, infiltrative proliferation composed predominantly of basaloid cells with focal squamous differentiation and an inflammatory infiltrate rich in plasma cells, lymphocytes, and melanophages. Neither specimen showed clear margins.

A contrast-enhanced CT scan demonstrated a 22 × 6 mm mass with ragged edges on the left cheek skin extending to the nasal skin. There was no invasion of adjacent bone or deeper adipose tissue and no skull metastases. No lymphadenopathy was seen in the neck or abdomen; a single mediastinal lymph node measured 1.5 cm. Fluid was noted in both maxillary sinuses; no free fluid was present in the abdomen or pelvis.

A 53-year-old woman with seropositive rheumatoid arthritis for 9 years, treated with methotrexate and leflunomide, presented with 2 weeks of pain and swelling in the right calf. She denied fever, cough, skin erythema, or prolonged immobility. Two years earlier, she had two similar episodes in the same calf, each treated with incision and drainage—yielding 1 liter of pus—and parenteral antibiotics, with poor recovery. On examination, her right calf was swollen and tender, the overlying skin was warm but not erythematous, and a scar from prior incision was present. Laboratory tests showed a white-cell count of 8200/μL with 75% neutrophils, an ESR of 114 mm/hr, a CRP of 33.4 mg/L, and normal procalcitonin. Blood, urine, and pus cultures (aerobic, anaerobic, fungal, and mycobacterial) were sterile; Gram and Ziehl–Neelsen stains were negative. Ultrasonography revealed a hypoechoic, loculated collection in the medial gastrocnemius muscle with no Doppler uptake and no thrombosis. MRI of the right leg demonstrated a 4 × 4 × 1.5 cm loculated collection in the medial head of the gastrocnemius, mild to moderate knee effusion, synovial thickening, and patchy marrow edema in the lateral femoral condyle.

A 21-year-old man presented to the emergency department in an altered state of consciousness. Six hours earlier, he had sniffed a drug of abuse, after which he developed a high-grade fever responsive to acetaminophen, two episodes of projectile vomiting, and within an hour of presentation progressed from irritability to stupor.

On examination, his temperature was elevated, he was hemodynamically stable, and his Glasgow Coma Scale score was 6 (E1 M4 V1). He had dilated, reactive pupils, an upgoing right plantar reflex, and no signs of meningeal irritation. A urine toxicology screen was positive for morphine and benzodiazepines. There was no history of head trauma, headaches, or abnormal movements.

Non-contrast CT of the head revealed diffuse sulcal effacement and hypoattenuation of the white matter. Laboratory studies showed a leukocyte count of 16.6 ×10^9/L with 82.3% neutrophils, hyponatremia (Na 128 mEq/L), hyperkalemia (K 5.8 mEq/L), urea 48 mg/dL, and creatinine 1.4 mg/dL. He had a history of pulmonary tuberculosis treated with antituberculosis therapy until one month before presentation.

A 53-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of progressive genu valgum and knee pain. Her family noted widening of the knees from age 8, which had gradually worsened without functional disability. Over the past year, knee pain increased and was unrelieved by analgesics. She had no visual, auditory, cognitive, or neurologic symptoms. Physical examination showed marked genu valgum, mild facial dysmorphism (prognathism, prominent ears, frontal bossing), and poor dental condition with caries and partial edentulism. There were no signs of anemia, jaundice, organomegaly, fractures, or neurologic deficits. Family history was notable for parental consanguinity and two of eight siblings with similar phenotypes; the parents were unaffected. Routine laboratory tests were within normal limits. A plain radiograph of the knees demonstrated marked femorotibial metaphyseal flaring with cortical thinning, consistent with an Erlenmeyer flask deformity.

A 31-year-old man with a history of asthma and obesity was referred for evaluation of a left breast mass. He first noticed the lesion approximately three years earlier; since then it had progressively enlarged and become painful. He had initially been evaluated in 2021 but was lost to follow-up. 

On presentation he was hemodynamically stable. Compared with the right breast, the left breast contained a large, soft, uniformly palpable mass. The overlying skin showed prominent veins, and the nipple was deviated inferiorly without discharge. There was no skin discoloration, dimpling, or thickening. He denied endocrine abnormalities, breast trauma, and a family history of breast disease. 

All laboratory tests, including routine blood work and tumor markers, were within normal limits. A diagnostic bilateral mammogram demonstrated a 20 cm encapsulated fatty mass occupying most of the left breast (BI-RADS category 4); the right breast was unremarkable. A focused ultrasound of the left breast showed a well-defined hypoechoic mass at the 12 o’clock position.

An 86-year-old man with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, a history of pulmonary tuberculosis treated in the 1950s, and localized prostate cancer presented with a 3-day history of productive cough, dyspnea, and wheezing. He denied fever, chills, chest pain, night sweats, or weight loss. He had a 40-pack-year smoking history, having quit more than 40 years ago.

On examination, he was afebrile, with normal blood pressure and heart rate but was tachypneic. A large, nonmobile, globular mass was visible under the skin in the left supraclavicular area. Lung auscultation revealed diffuse expiratory wheezing, bilateral basilar crackles, and diminished breath sounds over the left upper lobe. Laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 20.6×10^3/μL with a left shift and a hemoglobin level of 9.2 g/dL.

A chest radiograph showed a large, partially calcified mass projecting over the left lung apex, measuring approximately 16.5×10.0 cm. Chest CT demonstrated a peripherally calcified soft-tissue mass in the left upper chest wall measuring 9.4×11.8×14.7 cm, with destruction of the first through third ribs.

Differential diagnoses included osteosarcoma, metastatic prostate cancer, mesothelioma, and other mass lesions. A tissue biopsy was deemed necessary to establish a diagnosis.

A 65-year-old man presented with a 6-week history of right breast swelling that had been insidious in onset but rapidly increased in size. He had no pain or fever. His past history was notable for familial lipomatosis with multiple longstanding subcutaneous nodules. On examination, there was a non-tender, firm, 15 cm hemispherical mass occupying the entire right breast, with peau d’orange of the overlying skin and nipple distortion. The left breast was normal and there was no axillary lymphadenopathy. Multiple soft, non-tender lipomas were palpable on the trunk, and a large right-sided hydrocele was present. Baseline blood tests and chest radiography were normal.  
Breast ultrasound showed an encapsulated mass with areas of decreased echogenicity suggestive of sarcomatous change in a lipoma. MRI of the breast demonstrated mixed signal intensity with areas of enhancement similar to those seen in soft-tissue sarcomas. CT scanning excluded pulmonary metastases. Mammography revealed an enlarged, dense right breast containing a well-circumscribed soft-tissue mass occupying nearly the entire breast, with a rim of normal tissue peripherally. An image-guided core biopsy was inconclusive; the histological appearance was compatible with a wide differential diagnosis including fibromatosis, fasciitis, myofibroblastoma, dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans, leiomyoma, or peripheral nerve sheath tumour.

A 28-year-old woman presented with a 1-week history of arm and leg cramps, fatigue, and carpal spasms, with similar intermittent episodes over the past 2 years. She denied diuretic or laxative use, vomiting, diarrhea, polyuria, or polydipsia. There was no family history of similar symptoms. On examination, her blood pressure was 98/66 mm Hg without orthostatic changes; she appeared euvolemic. Chvostek’s sign and Trousseau’s sign were both positive. Laboratory tests showed hypocalcemia, hypomagnesemia, and hypokalemia. Arterial blood gas analysis revealed pH 7.51 (7.35–7.45), bicarbonate 33.5 mmol/L (22–30), PCO2 42 mm Hg (32–45), and chloride 86 mEq/L (102–109). Complete blood count, fasting glucose, creatinine, liver function tests, thyroid profile, rheumatoid factor, and antinuclear antibodies were normal. Renal ultrasonography showed no nephrocalcinosis. Urinalysis demonstrated a pH of 6.7 and a specific gravity of 1.018. Genetic studies were not available.

A 3-year-old boy presented with pain and swelling of the left hemiscrotum for 13 hours. Occasionally, enlargement of the left hemiscrotum without any associated symptoms had been observed. He had no nausea, vomiting, fever, or history of scrotal trauma. On examination, the left hemiscrotum was enlarged without erythema and contained a palpable, tender, cystic mass. The cremasteric reflex was present bilaterally, and the right testis was normal. Transillumination of the left hemiscrotum was negative. Urinalysis was normal. Ultrasonography revealed an encapsulated cystic mass with heterogeneous echogenicity near the normal left testis, and color Doppler imaging showed normal blood flow to the testis and epididymis bilaterally.

A 70-year-old man was referred for evaluation of recurrent urticaria and suspected repaglinide hypersensitivity. His medical history included hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and persistent rhinitis. He is a hunter and dog owner in continental Croatia, with multiple tick bites over the years. His chronic medications were metformin, insulin glargine, lercanidipine, and pantoprazole.

Seven years before referral, he experienced urticaria 6–8 h after ingesting simethicone, which was attributed to a drug allergy. Two months later—2 weeks after a tick bite—he had a second urticarial episode without an obvious trigger. He also reported generalized pruritus without rash after tamsulosin and mild urticarial rash after eating sausages. Notably, he regularly ate cooked red meat (beef, pork, and lamb) without any symptoms.

In the month before referral, he had three short-lived urticarial eruptions following fresh tick bites. The first two occurred 6–8 h after taking repaglinide and resolved with chloropyramine and methylprednisolone. The last episode, accompanied by left-sided facial angioedema, began one day before referral and worsened after taking fexofenadine. He had no respiratory, cardiovascular, or gastrointestinal symptoms.

On examination, he had generalized urticaria and left facial angioedema, which quickly resolved with chloropyramine and methylprednisolone. Initial laboratory studies showed leukocytosis (10.6×10^9/L) with neutrophilia (9.62×10^9/L), lymphopenia (0.72×10^9/L), and an elevated C-reactive protein (21.8 mg/L); other studies were pending.

A 32-year-old previously healthy man presented with a 2-week history of epileptic seizures. Initially, he had two episodes of focal aware seizures manifesting as left-arm convulsions lasting 2–3 seconds without altered sensorium. The next day, he had two similar focal aware seizures. On day 3, he experienced a generalized tonic–clonic seizure with eye deviation, limb jerking, tongue biting, and loss of consciousness lasting 1–2 minutes, followed by a 20-minute postictal state. He then noted paresthesia in the left extremities, and 1 week after onset he had a 2-minute episode of transient sensory loss in the left limbs. There was no history of trauma, infection, or febrile illness, and family history was unremarkable. Neurological examination was normal.

Electroencephalography showed sharp waves, sharp–slow complexes, and irregular δ waves originating from the bilateral temporal lobes, predominantly on the left. One week before admission, conventional MRI with spectroscopy and angiography was normal. Laboratory studies—including blood and urine cell counts, chemistry, coagulation profile, thyroid function, CSF routine and immuno-antibodies, tumor biomarkers, and screening for NMDA-R, CASPR2, AMPAR1, AMPAR2, LGI1, and GABAR-B2 antibodies—were all normal. On hospital day 2, repeat brain MRI revealed poorly defined bilateral frontoparietal lesions that were hypointense on T1-weighted images and hyperintense on T2-weighted, FLAIR, and STIR images. Antiepileptic therapy with oxcarbazepine was initiated, and the patient remained seizure-free thereafter.

A 45-year-old man was referred for evaluation of a retroperitoneal left adrenal mass that had been discovered incidentally on abdominal ultrasonography 3 months earlier. His medical history was notable only for mild hypertension on medication and an appendectomy 37 years earlier; he had no family history of endocrine or neoplastic disease. On examination, his abdomen was soft, with no palpable masses; there were no cutaneous lesions or lymphadenopathy. Routine blood tests were normal. Twenty-four–hour urinary catecholamines, cortisol, and chromogranin levels were all within normal limits. Ultrasonography showed a heterogeneous, expansive mass measuring 12 cm in maximal diameter adjacent to the left adrenal gland. A contrast-enhanced CT scan of the abdomen confirmed a 14 cm heterogeneous mass with both solid and cystic components arising from the left adrenal gland.

A 68-year-old man was transferred for further management after an inferior wall ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction. His medical history was notable for malignant fibrous histiocytoma of the right preauricular and left jugular regions, with regional relapse and a focal lung metastasis treated surgically, as well as essential hypertension, hypercholesterolemia, type II diabetes mellitus, and tobacco use. He also had a family history of coronary artery disease. On examination, his blood pressure was 135/85 mm Hg, and cardiac auscultation was normal without murmurs.

Electrocardiography showed normal sinus rhythm and inverted T waves in leads II, III, aVF, and V3–V6. A coronary angiogram revealed no critical stenoses of the coronary arteries. On the following day, he had multiple episodes of witnessed cardiac arrest due to polymorphic ventricular tachycardia, which required defibrillation. Routine laboratory tests, including electrolyte levels, were within normal limits. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated a 6.2 × 4.0 cm mass in the left ventricle, fixed to the interventricular septum.

Two healthy sisters, ages 25 and 27, presented in March 2020 with a 1‐month history of progressive facial pain, swelling, erythema, and fever that began 2 weeks after they each underwent autologous cryopreserved fat injection. Three months earlier, they had received fresh fat injections at the same clinic, followed by a session of cryopreserved fat injection 1 month later; both fresh and cryopreserved fat had been obtained and injected at home or in the same nonhospital setting. Patient 1 had been treated elsewhere with vancomycin, imipenem, and metronidazole for 10 days without improvement. Patient 2 had received ciprofloxacin, cefazolin, cloxacillin, cephalexin, and doxycycline, also without response. 

On examination, both were afebrile. Patient 1 had erythema, edema, and purulent ulcers on her cheeks, buccal regions, and chin, with an indurated area on the right buccal region and a fistula with pus in the right buccal mucosa. Patient 2 had multiple edematous facial nodules and an erythematous, purulent area lateral to the right nasolabial fold. 

Laboratory studies showed for Patient 1 a C‐reactive protein level of 20.0 mg/L (normal <3.0 mg/L) and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 55 mm/hr (normal 0–29 mm/hr), and for Patient 2 a C‐reactive protein level of 10.0 mg/L; other values were unremarkable. Facial ultrasound for Patient 1 revealed multiple collections of debris bilaterally along the lips, chin, zygomatic arches, and right zygomatic area, with increased fat echogenicity and hypoechoic septa suggestive of cellulitis. For Patient 2, ultrasound showed various debris collections under the left eyebrow, left cheek, inferior lip, left mandible, medially to the right eyebrow, and along the right zygomatic arch and nasolabial fold, with findings compatible with granuloma, panniculitis, and fat necrosis. 

Both patients underwent abscess drainage; Gram staining was negative. Empiric therapy with clindamycin, cefepime, acyclovir, levofloxacin, clarithromycin, and doxycycline failed to produce improvement. Suspecting nonautologous fat injection, both were treated with prednisolone (40 mg daily for 10 days) but showed no clinical response.

A 39-year-old man presented with right flank pain that progressed over two days to severe back pain, bilateral flank pain, scrotal pain, and bilateral thigh pain. He had no significant medical history. Analgesics administered at a local health center relieved his back pain, but his testicular and thigh pain worsened.  
On examination, blood pressure was 120/80 mmHg, pulse 130 beats/min, respiratory rate 20 breaths/min, and temperature 37°C. The leg-raising test was negative, and the neurovascular status of both legs was intact. Scrotal examination was unremarkable; both the cremasteric reflex and Prehn's sign were negative. There was no edema or swelling of the lower limbs.  
Laboratory studies showed a D-dimer level of 17 mg/L (normal, 0–0.49 mg/L), creatinine 124 µmol/L (normal, 64–110 µmol/L), white blood cell count 10×10^3/L (normal, 4–10×10^3/L), and C-reactive protein 5 mg/L (normal, 0–5 mg/L). Prothrombin time was 13.8 seconds (normal, 9.4–12.5 seconds), with normal APTT (28.4 seconds) and INR (1.1). Urinalysis was positive for blood.  
Bedside abdominal ultrasound showed no significant abnormalities. Doppler ultrasound of both legs revealed deep vein thrombosis extending from the external iliac veins to the distal veins of both legs. Computed tomography of the abdomen demonstrated a large thrombus in the distal inferior vena cava extending to the renal artery and both external and internal iliac veins, with no evidence of renal calculi or other abdominal pathology.

An 18-year-old woman from rural Australia with no past medical history presented with a 3-day history of a spreading, pruritic, papulovesicular eruption on both knees and hands, with preferential involvement of the right hand, as well as superficial crusting of the lips. Two weeks earlier she had been bitten on her right hand by her pet calf. She remained systemically well and denied fevers, arthralgias, new or regular medications, preceding illness, or exposure to sheep, goats, gardening, or fish tanks.  
On examination, there was a violaceous eroded nodule on the dorsal aspect of the right middle finger with an erythematous-yellow papulovesicular eruption; erythematous papules and nodules were present on the dorsal and palmar surfaces of the left hand and on both knees without pustules; superficial yellow crusting was seen on the lips; no bullae or lymphadenopathy were observed.  
Skin biopsy revealed inflammatory material and keratin debris with no microorganisms noted on PAS, Grocott, Ziehl–Neelsen, or Wade Fite stains.
predicted_diagnoses: ['orf (contagious pustular dermatosis)', 'dyshidrotic eczema', 'herpes simplex virus infection']

case_text: A 15‐year‐old Omani male presented with lifelong transfusion‐dependent anemia (since age 3; ~12 transfusions/year) and iron overload managed with chelation. His parents are first cousins; siblings are healthy. On examination, he was pale and mildly icteric, with dry skin. Vital signs were normal. Abdominal examination revealed a soft, non‐tender spleen palpable 7 cm below the costal margin and a liver edge 4 cm below the costal margin. Laboratory studies showed Hb 8.7 g/dL, MCV 75 fL, reticulocyte count 4.7%, undetectable haptoglobin (< 0.06 g/L), ferritin 1628 µg/L, total bilirubin 18–45 µmol/L, and LDH 543 U/L. Hemoglobin variant analysis revealed HbA 96.3%, HbA2 3.1%, and HbF 0.6%. The peripheral smear showed a dimorphic red‐cell picture, mild polychromasia, and numerous nucleated RBCs. Bone marrow examination demonstrated erythroid hyperplasia and dyserythropoiesis with megaloblastic features, binucleate and multinucleate erythroblasts, and cytoplasmic bridging. Given these findings, congenital dyserythropoietic anemia (CDA) was suspected, and sequencing of CDAN1 and C15ORF41 identified two rare variants in trans. However, the same genotype was present in an unaffected brother, and CDA was ruled out.

A 76-year-old man was referred for evaluation of an 18-month history of chronic nonbloody watery diarrhea and unintentional weight loss of approximately 57 kg. He noted progressive onycholysis of the hands and feet and hyperpigmentation of the palms, soles, legs, and abdomen over the same interval. He also reported hypogeusia, early satiety, chronic heartburn, and intermittent nonspecific abdominal discomfort. He denied fever, night sweats, or overt gastrointestinal bleeding. His medical history included prostate cancer treated in 2012, gout, a remote transient ischemic attack, osteoarthritis, and cataracts. An outpatient workup was negative for recurrent malignancy, autoimmune disease, celiac sprue, and pancreatic insufficiency. 

On examination, he appeared cachectic (weight 50.9 kg; BMI 16.5) with marked sarcopenia. He had nonscarring scalp alopecia; dystrophic nails on hands and feet; diffuse hyperpigmentation of the palms, dorsal fingers, face, and limbs; and loss of abdominal, axillary, and pubic hair. The abdomen was scaphoid and nontender. No lymphadenopathy was detected.

Laboratory studies showed a mild normocytic anemia (hemoglobin 119 g/L) and eosinophilia (0.82 g/L), with hypoalbuminemia (28 g/L). Electrolytes, liver and renal panels, inflammatory markers, immunoglobulins, and autoantibodies were within normal limits or undetectable. Stool occult blood was positive; stool cultures (including Clostridioides difficile) and malabsorption panels were negative.

Abdominal CT revealed extensive gastric and duodenal mucosal fold thickening. Upper endoscopy demonstrated “carpet-like” semipedunculated polyps measuring 5–20 mm throughout the stomach and duodenum with thickened folds. Histopathology of duodenal polyps showed edematous mucosa with variably dilated and branching glands, gastric foveolar metaplasia, blunted or absent villi, and a lamina propria infiltrate rich in eosinophils and mononuclear cells. Gastric polyps had elongated, irregular foveolar glands and marked lamina propria edema with eosinophils. Helicobacter organisms were not identified. The involvement of the duodenum and gastric antrum ruled out Menetrier’s disease, which is typically confined to the gastric body.

A 78-year-old man was referred for evaluation of an ulcerated lesion of the left retromolar trigone. He reported feeling “a hole in his gums” behind his lower left teeth; the duration was unknown. His medical history was notable only for glaucoma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. On examination, there was a 0.4-cm round papule with mixed red and white coloration and indurated borders on the left retromolar pad. Portions of the lesion had a vaguely verruciform (warty) white appearance. The lesion was mildly tender but otherwise asymptomatic, and the rest of the oral mucosa was unremarkable. A panoramic radiograph showed no bone involvement. The clinical differential diagnosis included chronic ulcer, exuberant granulation tissue, irritated papilloma, and squamous cell carcinoma. A biopsy of the lesion and surrounding mucosa was performed.

Histologic examination showed soft tissue covered by benign-appearing stratified squamous epithelium with overlying keratotic material. Within the surface epithelium, there was suprabasilar (intraepithelial) clefting and elongated, test-tube–shaped rete ridges. The lesion also exhibited basilar hyperplasia and dyskeratosis. A slender, keratin-filled crevice extended from the epithelial surface to the region of suprabasilar clefting.

A 13-year-old girl was referred for persistent right periorbital and frontal headache with vomiting. One month earlier she had a mild SARS-CoV-2 infection. Two days after headache onset, she developed dizziness, right gaze diplopia, and right upper-eyelid edema without fever or other neurologic signs. Her past development was unremarkable. On day 2, neurologic examination showed limited adduction and elevation of the right eye, right ptosis, and a dilated pupil consistent with a right third-cranial-nerve palsy. Endocrine evaluation revealed weight 73.8 kg (> 97th centile), BMI 33.6 kg/m2, hepatic steatosis on abdominal ultrasound, LDL cholesterol 129 mg/dL, HDL cholesterol 31 mg/dL, and otherwise normal blood tests. Noncontrast head CT was normal. Brain MRI with TOF angiography demonstrated linear thickening of the right tentorial profile with intense contrast enhancement extending from its anterior insertion and thickening of the lateral wall of the right cavernous sinus. CT angiography confirmed abnormal vessel thickening in the same region. A comprehensive blood work-up for viral and bacterial agents, thyroid dysfunction, and antiganglioside antibodies, and cerebrospinal fluid analysis including antiganglioside antibodies, were unremarkable.

A 16.5-year-old girl was admitted with uncontrolled vomiting, severe headache, and fever. A few hours before admission, she had injected methamphetamine and morphine intravenously, as she had done intermittently over recent weeks. 

Her history included recurrent childhood respiratory infections; allergic rhinitis and seasonal asthma since age 6; aseptic necrosis of the right tibial tuberosity at 11; adenoidectomy at 12; reflux esophagitis at 13; behavioral disorders with self-harm and experimentation with opioids and stimulants since 13; a suicide attempt at 15; and a diagnosis of bipolar disorder treated with aripiprazole, pregabalin, and quetiapine.

On examination: temperature 39.9 °C, heart rate 120–130 beats/min, respiratory rate 26/min, blood pressure 90/67 mmHg; fine tremors of all limbs, skin hyperalgesia, dehydration, dry mucous membranes, and mild abdominal tenderness.

Laboratory studies showed:  
• C-reactive protein 37.4 mg/L (normal < 5)  
• Procalcitonin 50.46 ng/mL (normal < 0.5)  
• Leukopenia (2.36 × 10^3/µL) with lymphopenia, monocytosis, eosinopenia  
• Capillary blood gas: hypocapnia  
• Elevated IgG, IgA, total IgE  
• Vitamin D 9.99 ng/mL  
Rapid SARS-CoV-2 antigen test was negative.

While awaiting toxicology, she received parenteral hydration and empirical cefuroxime.

A 61-year-old woman presented with a two-day history of left-sided throat pain, dysphagia, and odynophagia that she believed followed swallowing a fish bone. The pain, tender to palpation, radiated to her left jaw. She had no prior thyroid symptoms and no significant medical history. On examination, her vital signs were normal. The neck was supple with a full range of motion. There was pinpoint tenderness medial to the left sternocleidomastoid and tender left supraclavicular lymph nodes without enlargement or crepitus. Laboratory studies were notable only for a leukocytosis of 13,100 μL. A CT scan of the neck demonstrated a 2.9 cm × 0.02 cm radiopaque linear structure in the anterior wall of the cervical esophagus at C6–T1, with surrounding air, and an enlarged lymph node adjacent to the left thyroid lobe. Initial esophagogastroduodenoscopy showed slight left-lateral esophageal bulging but no foreign body. A repeat neonatal-scope EGD identified an umbilicated lesion 3 cm proximal to the upper esophageal sphincter; again, no foreign body was found. At this point, the differential diagnosis included a periesophageal abscess from esophageal perforation, an embedded fish bone, inflamed ectopic thyroid tissue, or malignancy.

A 58-year-old man presented with a 2-year history of a slowly growing mass in his right popliteal region, noting more rapid enlargement over the past 6 months. He reported mild pain and intermittent paresthesia of the right leg and foot. On examination, there was a 4 × 2 cm mobile, solid mass posterior to the knee without erythema, warmth, or ulceration. Tinel’s sign was positive over the mass. Sensory and motor function of the lower extremity were normal. Ultrasound imaging showed a solid mass separate from the adjacent muscles and tendons. Magnetic resonance imaging revealed a well-defined, solid, heterogeneous, dense mass originating from the tibial nerve.

A 78-year-old man presented with diffuse abdominal pain. He had undergone total excision of a dorsal cutaneous malignant melanoma 4 years earlier, with no evidence of metastatic disease at that time. On examination, he appeared well; his abdomen was soft, without palpable masses. There was no melena, and laboratory studies were within normal limits. Hepatobiliary ultrasound showed multiple hyperechoic nodules measuring at least 1 cm in the fundus, body, and neck of the gallbladder, with minimal to absent acoustic shadowing. The remainder of the abdomen was poorly visualized because of adiposity. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen confirmed these gallbladder lesions, which exhibited early intense enhancement followed by progressive washout, and revealed multiple solid masses up to 5 cm in the stomach, duodenum, and ileum with similar enhancement patterns, as well as diffuse peritoneal nodules, mesenteric lymphadenopathy, and pelvic ascites. Chest CT demonstrated bilateral solid pulmonary nodules without pleural effusion. The possibility of metastatic disease was considered.
predicted_diagnoses: ['gastrointestinal metastasis from cutaneous malignant melanoma', 'gallbladder metastasis from cutaneous malignant melanoma', 'gastrointestinal metastasis from unknown primary']

case_text: A 30-year-old man presented with progressive dyspnea, bilateral lower-limb swelling, and abdominal pain beginning three days after a honey bee sting on the dorsal aspect of his right foot. His only past medical history was a stab wound to the heart repaired two years earlier. On examination, blood pressure was 80/50 mmHg, pulse 120 beats/min, and oxygen saturation 89% on room air. Heart sounds were muffled, and breath sounds were decreased at both lung bases. The abdomen was distended with generalized tenderness and shifting dullness. The sting site was red with central necrosis. Laboratory tests showed a white blood cell count of 12,000/mm3; other studies were normal. Contrast CT of the chest and abdomen demonstrated massive pericardial effusion, bilateral pleural effusions, and ascites. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a normal left ventricular ejection fraction and massive pericardial effusion with signs of cardiac tamponade.

An 81-year-old man presented with sudden onset of right-sided abdominal wall bulging and sharp pain radiating in the Th11 dermatome. He denied leg pain, back pain, bladder or bowel dysfunction, or gait disturbances. Sensation was intact throughout, and there were no skin lesions. His medical history included a left middle cerebral artery stroke and hypertension. On examination, there was a visible bulge of the right abdominal wall and reproducible radicular tenderness in the Th11 distribution; the remainder of the neurologic and abdominal examinations was unremarkable. A CT scan of the abdomen was performed and showed no evidence of an abdominal wall hernia.

A 78-year-old man presented with a 2-week history of progressively worsening right cheek swelling, pain, and trismus. He denied any history of facial trauma or fever. His past medical history was notable only for prostate cancer treated with radiation therapy 2 years earlier; he was not on immunosuppressive medications and reported no exposures to birds or soil. On examination, there was marked swelling of the right zygomatic region with severe spontaneous pain and tenderness; maximal interincisal opening was approximately 2 cm. Laboratory studies revealed a C-reactive protein level of 0.45 mg/dL (reference range, 0–0.14), a white blood cell count of 6700/mm3 with 54.4% neutrophils, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 35 mm/h (reference range, 2–10). Routine chemistries, including alkaline phosphatase, calcium, phosphorous, glucose, HbA1c, and liver enzymes, were within normal limits. Serologic tests for hepatitis B and C were negative. Contrast-enhanced computed tomography of the face demonstrated a 30-mm lesion in the right zygomatic arch with bone destruction, surrounding contrast enhancement, and extension into the subcutaneous cheek tissue without calcification or periosteal reaction. These findings raised concern for a malignant tumor of the zygomatic bone or a focal abscess with atypical features.

A 38-year-old Caucasian woman presented with painful skin lesions on her bilateral upper and lower extremities and joint pain in her knees, wrists, and ankles. Two weeks earlier, she had an upper respiratory illness with sore throat, cough, congestion, and body aches that improved over 2–3 days with over-the-counter medications. She then traveled to Europe for 10 days; towards the end of that trip she developed joint pain. Two days prior to presentation, she noted the first skin lesion on her right wrist. 

She had no significant past medical history, was in generally good health, and had a negative tuberculin skin test one month earlier. She denied recent antibiotic use or birth control pills. Family history was notable for maternal hypothyroidism. She did not smoke and drank alcohol socially.

On examination, vital signs were stable. The posterior pharynx showed cobblestoning without cervical lymphadenopathy. Dermatologic exam revealed multiple raised, erythematous, tender nodules on the palms, forearms, anterior lower legs, and dorsal feet. There was decreased range of motion of the wrists and ankles due to pain; strength was 5/5 and reflexes were 2+ throughout.

Erythema nodosum was suspected. She was started on amoxicillin 875 mg twice daily for 10 days and ibuprofen 800 mg every 8 hours for pain. A CBC with differential, CMP, TSH, ASO titer, ESR, and chest radiograph were ordered, and she was instructed to return in one week.

A 31-year-old man with no significant past medical history or family history of cancer presented to the emergency department with four weeks of clot-forming gross hematuria. Five weeks earlier, he had an episode of asymptomatic gross hematuria that was treated as a urinary tract infection. On this admission he was in hypovolemic shock from ongoing hematuria. Vital signs showed tachycardia and diaphoresis. Lung auscultation revealed bilateral rales; the abdomen was soft with no palpable masses or peritoneal signs. Genital examination showed a normal penis and descended testes, but there was an indurated, painless mass in the left scrotal sac without erythema or warmth.  
Laboratory studies revealed a white-cell count of 7560/μL, hemoglobin 11.5 g/dL, platelets 204 000/μL; prothrombin time 16 s, partial thromboplastin time 33.2 s, INR 1.3; serum glucose 116 mg/dL; blood urea nitrogen 14 mg/dL; creatinine 1.3 mg/dL; sodium 134 mmol/L; potassium 3.5 mmol/L; chloride 102 mmol/L. Tumor markers were remarkable for a β-hCG of 483 100 mIU/mL, an α-fetoprotein level of 1.05 ng/mL, and an LDH of 386 IU/L.

case_text: A 13-year-old girl presented with a 7‐month history of right‐sided nasal obstruction, anosmia, intermittent epistaxis, snoring, and hearing loss. She denied trauma, anorexia, or weight loss. On examination, a mass occupied the right nasal cavity, displaced the septum toward the left, and extended into the nasopharynx, pushing the soft palate downward. Otoscopic examination revealed bilateral tympanic‐membrane retraction and dullness. Cranial nerves were intact, and there was no cervical lymphadenopathy. Hematologic and biochemical laboratory tests were within normal limits. Computed tomography of the paranasal sinuses showed complete opacification of the right nasal cavity, maxillary, ethmoidal, sphenoid, and frontal sinuses, with remodeling of the nasal septum toward the left side.

A 17-year-old previously healthy male was transferred for management of acute hydrocephalus and intraventricular hemorrhage. He had a 3-day history of headache responsive to paracetamol, followed on the day of transfer by severe headache, persistent vomiting, and a decreased level of consciousness. On arrival, his Glasgow Coma Scale score was 9 of 15; pupils were equal and reactive bilaterally, and there was spontaneous movement of all limbs.

Noncontrast head CT showed a well-defined, lobulated mass measuring 6.0 × 5.0 × 3.0 cm filling the frontal horn and body of the right lateral ventricle. The mass was predominantly hyperdense with heterogeneous attenuation and areas of calcification; there was no gross contrast enhancement. The lesion displaced the septum pellucidum and extended across midline by about 3 cm, causing obstructive hydrocephalus. Hyperdense blood was noted in the dependent portions of both lateral ventricles and in the third and fourth ventricles.

An external ventricular drain was placed emergently, yielding bloody cerebrospinal fluid under high pressure. Preoperative MRI of the brain revealed a heterogeneous mass approximately 40 mm in maximal diameter within the right lateral ventricle. The lesion was hypointense on both T1- and T2-weighted images and showed no contrast enhancement. Findings were consistent with an intraventricular tumor associated with hemorrhage, most pronounced in the right lateral ventricle.

A 68-year-old woman with a history of hysterectomy and a maternal history of Crohn’s disease was transferred for recurrent severe lower abdominal pain. Four weeks earlier, she had presented with constant lower abdominal pain without diarrhea, intermittent constipation, and a 4-pound weight loss. She denied smoking, alcohol, or drug use. 

Initial abdominal CT showed abnormal wall thickening and fat stranding within the terminal ileum consistent with an infectious or inflammatory process; mesenteric lymph nodes in the right lower quadrant were mildly prominent, and neoplastic disease could not be ruled out. Colonoscopy revealed normal mucosa except for superficial linear erosions in the distal rectum, sigmoid diverticulosis, and an 8 mm ascending colon polyp. The terminal ileum appeared diffusely congested without ulcerations; biopsy specimens from the ileocecal valve showed no pathology. 

Laboratory studies demonstrated a hemoglobin of 12 g/L, hematocrit 37.5%, WBC 10.2 cells/mcL, platelets 338 cells/mcL, normal serum chemistries, C-reactive protein 45 mg/L, and calprotectin 408 µg/mL. Tuberculosis T-spot and hepatitis panels were negative.

She was diagnosed with Crohn’s disease and treated with intravenous methylprednisolone, transitioned to oral prednisone, and discharged. Ten days later, she returned with recurrent pain; a repeat CT was similar, and she was treated with ciprofloxacin, metronidazole, and a prednisone taper with planned outpatient infliximab. Two days after discharge, she again developed worsening abdominal pain. A third CT showed terminal ileal wall thickening, fat stranding, free fluid, ileus, and a possible abscess or phlegmon. 

On transfer, she was hemodynamically stable and afebrile, with ongoing abdominal pain but no nausea, diarrhea, bleeding, fever, or chills. Labs again showed slight anemia with otherwise normal counts and chemistries. Gastroenterology performed a repeat colonoscopy with biopsy of the terminal ileum; histology revealed only mild inflammatory changes and congestion.
